• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys reversed verb register inside symbolic action — "Out came Napoleon himself, majestically upright, casting haughty glances from side to side, and with his dogs gambolling round him. He carried a whip in his trotter" (Chapter 10) — combining reversed verb register (the verb "gambolling" applied to the dogs that had previously "dashed" and "baying", the apparatus of terror domesticated into pageantry within a single verb-shift) with symbolic action (the whip in the trotter condensing the entire allegory's argument into a single visual image, the dogs the regime's permanent courtiers).

• Close analysis: The verb "gambolling" registers the institutional violence as the regime's permanent pet — the threat no longer requiring the verbs of attack because the lesson of Chapter 7 has rendered the threat structural — while the prepositional phrase "round him" installs the dogs spatially as Napoleon's court, the geography enacting the courtly normalisation the verb has already announced.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the violence's consummation as visual furniture — the dogs domesticated into pageantry the form of a regime whose enforcement has become so embedded that it no longer requires the spectacles of Chapter 7 to operate.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the controlling concept's most lethal implication — that the regime's success is measured by the invisibility of its enforcement, and that the dogs "gambolling" are the regime's announcement that the citizens whose silence it has secured can no longer name the threat as threat.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the regime's enforcement instrument is most lethal when it has become routine — that the dogs at the closing dinner are the regime's announcement that the violence has been institutionalised into the regime's permanent visual furniture, and that the citizens who watch the gambolling without flinching have been trained into the precise condition the controlling concept names.

• Context (AO3): By the late 1940s the NKVD had been reorganised into the MGB and would, after 1954, become the KGB — the spectacular show trials of the 1930s replaced by the routine bureaucratic terror Solzhenitsyn would document in The Gulag Archipelago (1973), the institutional invisibility the closing chapters of Animal Farm anticipate.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell uses the dogs to anatomise the apparatus of fear and control — Chapter 3 clandestine cultivation, Chapters 5 and 7 terrorising deployment, Chapter 10 courtly normalisation — three stages of the same enforcement instrument the regime requires from its first months onward.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the puppies' secret education, Chapters 5 and 7 escalated as the throat-tearing on the killing knoll, Chapter 10 consummated as the dogs "gambolling" at the closing dinner — three operations of the same apparatus, the consistency confirming Orwell's claim that the regime's enforcement is not the regime's accessory but its institutional spine.

• Author's purpose: Writing from his own Spanish experience of being hunted by Stalinist forces through Barcelona in 1937, Orwell may be insisting that the regime's enforcement instrument is the citizen's first object of vigilance — that the dogs are recognisable by the loft they were trained in long before the brass-studded collars arrive in the barn.

• Ultimate moral: The dogs' enduring force as fable creations is that the reader watches them assembled and remembers, when they finally gambol at the closing dinner, the puppies they once were — and Orwell's pedagogy is to train the reader to recognise the cultivation before the deployment, the analytical move the citizens of Animal Farm did not have the time to learn.

C6 — Mr Jones

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Mr Jones to explore ideas about tyranny and continuity in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Mr Jones

- how Orwell uses Mr Jones to explore ideas about tyranny and continuity.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Mr Jones is the pre-revolutionary tyrant whose drunken negligence opens the novella and whose name is wielded for ten chapters as the regime's permanent rhetorical justification — "Surely, comrades, you would not want Jones back?" The plan tracks three stages — Jones as the original tyrant the revolution overthrows, Jones as the failed counter-revolutionary at the Battle of the Cowshed, Jones as the rhetorical absence the pigs invoke to license every betrayal until the closing dinner reveals the new tyranny indistinguishable from the old.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — Jones is simultaneously Tsar Nicholas II (abdicated March 1917) AND the universal political archetype of the deposed tyrant the new regime requires as permanent rhetorical bogey. Land in BP3 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Mr Jones = Tsar Nicholas II, the autocrat whose incompetence drove the February Revolution of 1917; the Manor Farm = pre-revolutionary Russia.

• Bathetic specificity — the proper noun "Mr. Jones, of the Manor Farm" registering the master in the bureaucratic register of land ownership.

• Rhetorical question — Squealer's "Surely, comrades, you would not want Jones back?" converting dissent into nostalgia for tyranny.

• Cyclical structure — Manor Farm renamed Animal Farm renamed Manor Farm; the form is the continuity.

• Symbolic naming — the farm is renamed "The Manor Farm" in Chapter 10, the regime announcing the cycle's closure through Jones's original noun.

• Failed counter-revolution — the Battle of the Cowshed (Chapter 4) the historical parallel to the Russian Civil War of 1918–22.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Tsar Nicholas II, who ruled the Russian Empire from 1894 until his abdication in March 1917, was a drunkard's son who inherited an autocracy he was temperamentally and politically incompetent to administer — his refusal to feed the urban workforce during the First World War, his repressive response to the 1905 Revolution, and his abdication after the February strikes the immediate historical occasion for the Bolshevik takeover in October 1917.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had observed Stalinist forces reproduce Tsarist methods in Barcelona in 1937 and who watched Stalin adopt Tsarist trappings — military uniforms, palatial residences, hierarchical titles — across the late 1930s and 1940s, opens Animal Farm with Mr Jones drunk in the farmhouse not because the pre-revolutionary tyranny was insignificant but because the cyclical structure requires the original tyrant whose specific operation the closing dinner restores.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 1 presentation of Jones as the original tyrant whose drunken negligence supplies the immediate occasion for revolution, the Chapter 4 staging of Jones as the failed counter-revolutionary at the Battle of the Cowshed, and the Chapters 5–10 invocation of Jones as the regime's permanent rhetorical bogey through Squealer's "Surely you would not want Jones back?" and the closing-chapter renaming of the farm as "The Manor Farm", Orwell uses Mr Jones to dramatise the controlling concept's most lethal claim — that tyranny is continuous across regimes, the pre-revolutionary master and the post-revolutionary pigs ultimately indistinguishable.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 1: Jones as the original tyrant (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Jones's drunken neglect; locking the hen-houses but failing to shut the popholes; Old Major's address against the human master.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Jones not as the diffuse pre-revolutionary background but as the specific operation of one master's control over the animal majority — his drunken negligence, his failure to feed the animals, and his absent administration the case the revolution is engineered to overthrow.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys lucid prose paired with bathetic specificity — the opening sentence "Mr. Jones, of the Manor Farm, had locked the hen-houses for the night, but was too drunk to remember to shut the popholes" (Chapter 1) — combining lucid prose (the windowpane register, the short Anglo-Saxon vocabulary, the absence of qualification or metaphor) with bathetic specificity (the proper noun "Mr. Jones, of the Manor Farm" registering the master in the bureaucratic register of land ownership, the closing detail "too drunk to remember to shut the popholes" exposing the negligence the revolution will subsequently overthrow) inside the same opening line.

• Close analysis: The conjunction "but" in "but was too drunk" registers the structural contradiction the revolution will name — the master who has the responsibility he has not earned and is incompetent to fulfil — while the proper noun "Mr. Jones, of the Manor Farm" establishes the bureaucratic register that makes the inequality invisible to those who benefit from it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the lucid prose of the opening register the tyranny as the structural condition rather than personal vice — the inequality the consequence of Jones's office, not Jones's character.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the controlling concept's foundation — the tyranny the revolution will overthrow is genuine, the hierarchy that produces Jones is structural, and the case for revolution is therefore honourable.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the pre-revolutionary tyranny is not a misunderstanding to be cleared up but a structural condition to be dismantled — and that the revolution's case is therefore not refutable by pointing to the betrayal that follows it; Jones is the original tyrant whose existence justifies the original revolution.

• Context (AO3): Tsar Nicholas II ruled the Russian Empire from 1894 until the February Revolution of 1917; his administration's incompetence in feeding the urban workforce during the First World War, its repressive response to the 1905 Revolution, and the autocratic structure of the Romanov state form the historical pattern Orwell condenses into Mr Jones's drunken neglect of the popholes.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 4: Jones as failed counter-revolutionary (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the Battle of the Cowshed; Jones and the men from neighbouring farms attempt to retake Manor Farm; Snowball leads the defence and earns "Animal Hero, First Class".

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 1 had installed Jones as the original tyrant, Chapter 4 stages him as the failed counter-revolutionary — the original master returning to retake the farm and defeated at the Battle of the Cowshed, the historical parallel to the Russian Civil War the structural beat the cyclical novella requires.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys understated narration paired with the catalogue of failure — "Mr. Jones and his men, with half a dozen others from Foxwood and Pinchfield, had entered the five-barred gate and were coming up the cart-track that led to the farm. They were all carrying sticks, except Jones, who was marching ahead with a gun in his hands" (Chapter 4) — combining understated narration (the lucid prose registering the counter-revolution as procedural attempt rather than righteous reclamation, the absence of heroic register exposing the bankruptcy of Jones's claim) with the catalogue of failure (the "sticks" and the single "gun" registering the inadequacy of Jones's resources, the syntax cataloguing the limits of the counter-revolution it is describing) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The proper nouns "Foxwood and Pinchfield" (the neighbouring farms) register the regional support Jones musters — the historical parallel to the foreign interventions during the Russian Civil War — while the comparative "except Jones, who was marching ahead with a gun" singles Jones out as the figure whose office requires the single firearm, the syntax exposing the lonely armed master in the position of the historical Tsar.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the defeat of Jones as the revolution's structural confirmation — the original tyrant's failed return validating the original revolution, the controlling concept's case for overthrow registering as historically accurate.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the structural beat the cyclical novella requires — the counter-revolution must fail in Chapter 4 so that the new tyranny can become, in Chapter 10, indistinguishable from the original; the failure of Jones is the precondition of the indistinguishability that the closing dinner will reveal.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the failure of the original counter-revolution is not the revolution's triumph but the precondition of the controlling concept's most lethal claim — that the regime which has defeated the original tyrant becomes, by the structural logic of unaccountable revolution, the new version of the tyranny it defeated.

• Context (AO3): The Russian Civil War of 1918–22 saw the Red Army defeat the White Russian counter-revolutionary forces (backed by British, French, American and Japanese expeditionary forces) — Trotsky's leadership of the Red Army the historical parallel to Snowball's leadership at the Cowshed; the failure of the counter-revolution then opened the structural opportunity for the new tyranny Stalin would build across the 1920s and 1930s.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 5–10: Jones as rhetorical absence (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: Squealer's "Surely, comrades, you would not want Jones back?" (Chapters 3, 5, 6, 8, 9); the closing-chapter renaming of the farm as "The Manor Farm".

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 4 had defeated Jones physically, Chapters 5–10 invoke him rhetorically — Squealer wielding Jones's name as the regime's permanent justification for every appropriation, every rewriting, every execution, the absent original tyrant the rhetorical instrument by which the new tyranny licenses itself.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys rhetorical question paired with cyclical renaming — Squealer's recurring "Surely, comrades, you would not want Jones back?" (Chapter 3 onwards) and the closing announcement "the name 'Animal Farm' had been abolished. Henceforward the farm was to be known as 'The Manor Farm' — which, he believed, was its correct and original name" (Chapter 10) — combining rhetorical question (the negative construction "would not want" pre-emptively shaming dissent, the rhetorical question converting opposition to the regime into nostalgia for Jones) with cyclical renaming (the farm restored to its pre-revolutionary noun, the regime's most audacious linguistic move announcing the cycle's completion through Jones's original name).

• Close analysis: The adverb "Surely" registers the technique's efficacy precisely because it pre-applies the answer the question expects — the syntax of the question itself eliminating the cognitive space in which the alternative could be considered — while the adjective "original" in "its correct and original name" exposes the regime's most audacious linguistic move, the pre-revolutionary tyranny rebranded as the natural state and the revolution itself rewritten as deviation.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the rhetorical efficiency of the technique — Jones's name supplying the negative pole that licenses every transgression of the regime's positive principles, the absent tyrant the most economical instrument of the new tyranny's continuing legitimacy.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the controlling concept's terminal claim — that the new tyranny requires the absent original tyrant to license itself, that the regime which has overthrown Jones requires Jones's permanent invocation, and that the renaming of the farm in Chapter 10 is the regime's announcement that the continuity has been achieved.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the unaccountable revolution does not destroy the original tyranny but reproduces it under new vocabulary — that Jones and Napoleon are the same structural position occupied by different bodies, and that the closing renaming of the farm is the regime's most lethal linguistic move because it announces, in the pre-revolutionary tyrant's original noun, that the cycle has completed.

• Context (AO3): Stalin adopted Tsarist trappings — military uniforms, palatial residences, hierarchical titles — across the late 1930s and 1940s; the November 1943 Tehran Conference saw him received as co-architect of the post-war order alongside Churchill and Roosevelt; the closing-chapter renaming of the farm as "The Manor Farm" condenses the historical pattern of Stalinist Russia's reproduction of Tsarist hierarchy under new vocabulary into a single sentence.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell uses Mr Jones to dramatise tyranny's continuity across regimes — Chapter 1 the original tyrant, Chapter 4 the failed counter-revolutionary, Chapters 5–10 the rhetorical absence whose invocation licenses every transgression and whose original noun the regime restores in the closing chapter.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 1 installed as the drunken master's structural negligence, Chapter 4 defeated as the failed counter-revolution, Chapters 5–10 invoked as the permanent rhetorical bogey and finally restored as the renamed Manor Farm — three operations of the same continuity, the consistency confirming Orwell's claim that the unaccountable revolution reproduces the tyranny it overthrew under new vocabulary.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1945 readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell may be using Jones to argue that the citizens of any post-revolutionary regime must be alert to the rhetorical invocation of the defeated tyrant — that the regime which keeps the absent original tyrant in permanent rhetorical view is the regime that requires that absence to license its own continuing operations.

• Ultimate moral: Jones's enduring force as a fable creation is that his name outlives his body for nine chapters before the renaming of the farm restores his original noun — and Orwell's pedagogy is to train the reader to recognise the rhetorical operation that converts defeated tyranny into the new tyranny's permanent justification, the analytical move the citizens of Animal Farm did not have the literacy to make.

C7 — Clover

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Clover to explore ideas about the silent witness in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Clover

- how Orwell uses Clover to explore ideas about the silent witness.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Clover is the novella's secondary protagonist and the reader's surrogate — the cart-horse who sees the rewriting in real time, doubts her memory under the pressure of the regime's narrative, and grieves Boxer at the close. The plan tracks three stages — Chapter 2 the maternal witness, Chapter 6 the doubting reader, Chapter 9 the silent griever whose recognition is the controlling concept registered in a single body.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — Clover is simultaneously the literate-but-disempowered Soviet working class woman of the 1920s–40s AND the universal political archetype of the witness whose memory the regime needs to discredit. The rewriting-history mechanism — Clover's softened "surely" in Chapter 6 is the regime's victory registered in the syntax of doubt itself. Land in BP2 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with Clover's consciousness; the modal "surely" tracking her cognitive defeat.

• Maternal symbolism — Clover's protective gathering of the smaller animals in Chapter 7 (the executions).

• Foil pairing — Clover against Mollie: the working-class witness against the working-class defector.

• Limited literacy — Clover can read "only the first letters" (Chapter 4), the literacy gap the regime requires.

• Silent grief — Clover at Boxer's van (Chapter 9); the wordless witness of the working class's consumption.

• Cyclical recognition — Clover at the closing dinner (Chapter 10) sees the indistinguishability the reader sees.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Soviet women supplied an estimated forty-three per cent of the industrial workforce during the Second Five-Year Plan (1933–37), worked the wartime Soviet factories that armed the Red Army during the Great Patriotic War (1941–45), and were celebrated in Pravda as the regime's moral spine — yet were systematically excluded from the Politburo and from the historical record the Short Course (1938) installed as the regime's only permitted narrative.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who in "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946) would diagnose the linguistic operation that empties the citizen's cognitive equipment of the standards by which the regime could be measured, constructs Clover as the working-class witness whose memory is the regime's most dangerous remaining threat and whose limited literacy is the gap through which the regime's rewriting operates.

• Thesis: Across her Chapter 2 maternal witness during the codification of the Seven Commandments, her Chapter 6 doubting recognition when the commandments are rewritten and her softened "surely" registers the regime's victory inside her own syntax, and her Chapter 9 silent grief at Boxer's van, Orwell presents Clover as the secondary protagonist and the reader's surrogate — the working-class witness whose silent recognition is the controlling concept registered in a single body.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 2: the maternal witness (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Clover at the Seven Commandments' first painting on the barn wall; her "motherly" gathering of the ducklings under her foreleg during Old Major's address.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Clover not as the revolution's articulator but as its maternal witness — the cart-horse whose body sheltered the ducklings under Major's address now standing before the public text of the commandments, the working-class loyalty present at the institutional moment but excluded from the institutional design.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys maternal characterisation paired with limited literacy — "a stout motherly mare approaching middle life, who had never quite got her figure back after her fourth foal" (Chapter 1) and the later "Clover learnt the whole alphabet, but could not put words together" (Chapter 3) — combining maternal characterisation (the adjective "motherly" and the parenthetical "never quite got her figure back" establishing Clover as the working-class woman whose body has already given) with limited literacy (the verbs "learnt" and "could not put words together", the close-analysis grammar registering the literacy gap the regime requires) inside the same characterisation.

• Close analysis: The pluperfect "had never quite got her figure back" registers Clover's body as the working-class woman's accumulated cost — the four foals already invisible in the regime that subsequently consumes them — while the conjunction "but" in "learnt the whole alphabet, but could not put words together" exposes the literacy gap precisely: Clover has the components but not the synthesis the regime requires her to lack.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels Clover's structural position as the working-class witness — the body already given before the revolution arrives, the literacy already incomplete before the commandments are written.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the regime's precondition — that the masses who built the revolution lack the literacy to defend its principles against the regime's subsequent rewriting, and that Clover is therefore the working class whose memory the regime will need to discredit.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the working class's exclusion from the institutional design is the gap the regime will exploit — that the codification of the principles without the distribution of the literacy is the gap through which the rewriting operates, and that Clover is the structural figure that gap produces.

• Context (AO3): Soviet women's literacy rose from roughly twenty per cent in 1917 to over ninety per cent by 1939 under the literacy campaigns of the 1920s, yet the literacy distributed was, by the late 1930s, literacy in the regime's preferred narrative — the Short Course (1938) effectively monopolised the historical record across the Soviet educational system, the literacy gap Orwell anatomises in Clover the historical pattern the campaigns produced rather than closed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 6: the doubting reader (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the commandments are rewritten; Clover notices the changes and asks Muriel to read them; her softened "surely" registers the regime's victory.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 2 had installed Clover as the maternal witness, Chapter 6 stages her as the doubting reader — the cart-horse who notices the rewriting, asks Muriel to read the wall, and registers the regime's victory in the softened modal "surely" that converts her confident memory into the tentative concession the rewriting requires.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys free indirect discourse inside the modal of defeat — "Surely there had been something in the Fourth Commandment about no animal sleeping in a bed? At any rate, Muriel was able to spell it out for her. 'It says, "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets"'" (Chapter 6) — combining free indirect discourse (Clover's interior thought presented as direct narrative, the modal "surely" registering her cognitive defeat from inside her own consciousness) with the verb "spell it out" (Muriel's slow letter-by-letter reading exposing the literacy gap that licenses the rewriting) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The modal "surely" performs the regime's victory in the syntax of doubt itself — the working-class memory softened into concession the moment the public text contradicts it — while the closing "At any rate" registers the surrender, the conjunction conceding the contest before the contest has been articulated.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the modal of defeat from inside Clover's mind — the cognitive vertigo the regime engineers registering as Clover's own voice rather than as Squealer's lie, the technique enacting the operation it is describing.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the regime's most lethal achievement — that the rewriting operates not by overpowering memory but by producing doubt, the working-class witness defeated not by the lie but by her own softened concession to the lie's plausibility.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the regime's central instrument is the production of doubt in the witness's own consciousness — that a regime which has captured the public text and the literacy required to challenge it is a regime that the working class cannot resist because the resistance would require the literacy the regime has withheld.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record; survivors who had attended the meetings the photographs depicted reported feeling that their own memories had become unreliable — the historical pattern of cognitive surrender Orwell condenses into Clover's softened "surely".

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 9: the silent griever (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Boxer's collapse; Clover at the side of the van; her wordless grief at the closing dinner.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 6 had installed Clover as the doubting reader, Chapter 9 stages her as the silent griever — the working-class woman whose body has already given accompanying the working-class body the regime has finally consumed, the maternal witness present at every stage of the controlling concept's exposition and without the linguistic equipment to name what she has seen.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys free indirect discourse inside silent recognition — "Fools! Fools!' shouted Benjamin, prancing round them and stamping the earth with his small hoofs. 'Fools! Do you not see what is written on the side of that van?'" and immediately Clover "gave a terrible cry which I had never heard" (Chapter 9) — combining free indirect discourse (Benjamin's words supplying the literacy Clover lacks, the close-analysis grammar exposing the literacy gap as the regime's instrument) with paralinguistic grief (Clover's "terrible cry", the wordless register of working-class grief the regime has produced without permitting it to name itself) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The exclamatory "Fools! Fools!" registers Benjamin's recognition supplying Clover's missing literacy — the structural foil of the cynic-who-knows-but-refuses-to-speak finally speaking for the witness-who-cannot-read — while Clover's "terrible cry" converts the controlling concept into pre-linguistic sound, the grief the regime has produced as its by-product registering at the level of breath rather than language.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels Clover's grief as the working-class woman's accumulated cost finally registering — the foals, the literacy gap, the rewritten commandments, the consumed Boxer all collapsing into the single cry that exits her body.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the structural form of the working-class woman's tragedy — that the grief the regime produces cannot be named in the language the regime has captured, and that Clover's wordless cry is therefore the most accurate utterance the novella permits her.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the working class's witness is the regime's most dangerous remaining threat — and that the regime's instrument of choice is therefore the literacy gap that converts witness into wordlessness, the cry that names nothing because the language has been withheld.

• Context (AO3): Soviet workers' grief at the deaths of family members in industrial accidents, gulag executions, and the Ukrainian famine of 1932–33 was, across the 1930s and 1940s, systematically denied public utterance — the Pravda obituary registers, the official funeral procedures, the regime's preferred narrative of accidental death all withholding the language by which the working class could have named the loss, the historical pattern Orwell condenses into Clover's "terrible cry".

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell constructs Clover as the secondary protagonist and the reader's surrogate — Chapter 2 the maternal witness, Chapter 6 the doubting reader, Chapter 9 the silent griever — three stages of the working-class woman whose memory the regime needs to discredit and whose grief the regime needs to withhold from language.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 2 installed as the maternal body already given, Chapter 6 defeated through the softened "surely" that produced doubt, Chapter 9 consummated through the "terrible cry" the regime had emptied of language — three stages of the same operation, the working-class witness's cognitive equipment progressively withdrawn until grief itself can no longer name what has been lost.

• Author's purpose: Writing the same year as "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), Orwell uses Clover to dramatise, at the level of fictional incident, the linguistic operation the essay analyses — the citizen's defence of clear thought as the prophylactic against the regime that requires the witness's wordlessness.

• Ultimate moral: Clover's enduring force is that the reader's grief at her grief is the reader's recognition of the controlling concept — the working-class witness whose memory the regime has discredited the figure the reader is being trained to defend, the analytical move that protects the witness from the regime that has captured her language.

C8 — Old Major

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Old Major to explore ideas about revolution and vision in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Old Major

- how Orwell uses Old Major to explore ideas about revolution and vision.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Old Major is the visionary diagnosis whose hamartia is the originating cause of everything that follows — the failure to specify, before the revolution, the mechanisms by which post-revolutionary leadership would be held accountable. The plan tracks three stages — Chapter 1 the diagnosis articulated and the anthem taught, the immediate aftermath of his death, the gap his absence leaves that the pigs occupy.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — Old Major condenses Marx (the diagnosis published 1848) AND Lenin (the call to action 1917) into a single boar AND the universal political archetype of the visionary theorist whose hamartia is the unspecified accountability mechanism. Land in BP2 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Hamartia — error in action (Aristotle, Poetics); Old Major's failure to specify accountability mechanisms is the hamartia the rest of the novella enacts.

• Visionary speech — Major's Chapter 1 address as the textbook case of an announced controlling concept.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses ("Man is the only…").

• Tricolon — three escalating items ("All men are enemies. All animals are comrades" expanded in the address).

• Anthem / collective song — "Beasts of England" as the founding emotional expression of the revolution.

• Allegorical naming — Marx (the diagnosis) + Lenin (the call to action), with the title "Major" signalling military-pedagogical authority.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Karl Marx published The Communist Manifesto in February 1848, diagnosing capitalism as the exploitation of the working class by the owners of capital; Vladimir Lenin returned to Russia in April 1917 from exile in Zurich, his arrival converting the diagnosis into a call to action that the October Revolution then attempted to enact.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell's Old Major condenses both figures into a single boar — Marx for the diagnosis articulated in the Chapter 1 address, Lenin for the anthem and the call to action — and his death three nights later, in advance of the revolution itself, is the structural parallel to Lenin's death in January 1924 that left the Soviet succession open to a leadership Marx had not anticipated.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 1 articulation of the diagnosis in the address to the assembled animals, his teaching of "Beasts of England" as the revolution's founding emotional expression, and the structural absence of any accountability mechanism in his vision that the pigs subsequently occupy, Orwell presents Old Major as the visionary whose diagnosis is honourable and whose hamartia — the failure to specify how power would be checked once the original visionary left the stage — is the originating cause of the betrayal that consumes the rest of the novella.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 1: the diagnosis articulated (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Old Major addresses the assembled animals in the big barn by candlelight.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Old Major's diagnosis as the moral baseline of the novella — the articulated case for revolution against which every subsequent betrayal will be measured — the address itself the textbook case of an announced controlling concept that the rest of the text will dramatise.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys anaphora inside catalogued accusation — Old Major's "Man is the only creature that consumes without producing. He does not give milk, he does not lay eggs, he is too weak to pull the plough, he cannot run fast enough to catch rabbits. Yet he is lord of all the animals" (Chapter 1) — combining anaphora (the repeated "He does not… he does not… he is too weak… he cannot" sequence cataloguing human incapacity through grammatical accumulation) with antithesis (the catalogue of incapacity set against the closing "Yet he is lord of all the animals", the contradiction supplying the revolutionary case in a single rhetorical step) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "he" repeated through the catalogue refuses Man the dignity of personhood, returning the linguistic violence Man has performed on the animals back through the syntax — while the conjunction "Yet" names the structural injustice in a single monosyllabic word, the smallness of the conjunction matched only by the size of the case it carries.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the case for revolution articulated with the lucid force of authentic political analysis — the speech persuasive in itself, the persuasiveness essential to the controlling concept Orwell is defending.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise structural choice — the diagnosis is honourable, and the betrayal that follows is therefore not the refutation of the original case but the warning that the case must be defended against the leadership it produces.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be insisting through Major's address that anti-Stalinism and anti-socialism are not the same position — that the diagnosis Marx published in 1848 retains its force regardless of the betrayal Stalin enacted, and that the reader who confuses the two has misread both the speech and the novella.

• Context (AO3): Karl Marx's The Communist Manifesto (February 1848) opens with "a spectre is haunting Europe" and proceeds through a structural analysis of capitalist exploitation — the address Old Major delivers in Chapter 1 condenses the Manifesto's diagnostic move into a single page, and Orwell's self-description as "democratic socialist" ("Why I Write", 1946) confirms the diagnosis is not what Orwell is satirising.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 1 closing: the anthem and the gap (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Old Major teaches "Beasts of England"; the animals sing it five times before sleep.

• Topic sentence: Where the address had installed the diagnosis, the closing of Chapter 1 installs the emotional form — "Beasts of England" as the revolution's founding song, the collective singing the moment the diagnosis becomes shared political feeling, and the gap the song does not address (what happens to the leadership after the revolution) the structural omission Orwell will spend the next nine chapters dramatising.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys collective ritual paired with prophetic register — Old Major teaches "Beasts of England" and "the whole farm burst out into Beasts of England in tremendous unison… they sang it right through five times in succession" (Chapter 1) — combining collective ritual (the five-fold singing converting the diagnosis into shared political identity) with prophetic register (the lyric "Tyrant Man shall be o'erthrown / And the fruitful fields of England / Shall be trod by beasts alone", the future tense announcing the revolution as inevitable) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The intensifier "tremendous unison" names the achievement of collective political feeling — the rare moment when the diagnosis and the masses align — while the ordinal "five times in succession" registers the depth of the commitment, the repetition both the strength of the moment and the absence of any accompanying institutional design.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the warmth of the collective revolutionary moment and the foreboding of its fragility — the song's emotional completeness only sharpening the recognition that the song specifies no mechanism by which the leadership it celebrates will be checked.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader perceives Old Major's hamartia not as personal failure but as the structural omission Orwell will diagnose retrospectively — the visionary's articulation of what the revolution must overthrow lacking the equally specific articulation of how the post-revolutionary leadership will be prevented from reproducing what was overthrown.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the visionary's job is not merely to diagnose the disease but to specify, in advance, the prophylactic — and that any vision that names the enemy without naming the accountability mechanism is the precondition of the regime that subsequently betrays the vision.

• Context (AO3): Lenin's State and Revolution (1917) attempted to specify post-revolutionary structures but its democratic-centralism framework was, by Lenin's own death in January 1924, already converging on the bureaucratic centralism Stalin would weaponise — the gap Orwell dramatises in Old Major's address is the historical gap State and Revolution failed to close.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — the gap occupied (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Old Major dies three nights after his address; the pigs immediately organise the principles into Animalism; the milk and apples are appropriated within hours of the revolution.

• Topic sentence: Where the address and the anthem had installed the diagnosis and the feeling, Old Major's death three nights later opens the structural gap the rest of the novella enacts — the pigs' immediate organisation of Animalism, the appropriation of the milk and apples within hours of the revolution, and the eventual displacement of the diagnosis by Squealer's propaganda all consequences of the gap the visionary did not close.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys structural absence inside narrative compression — "Three nights later old Major died peacefully in his sleep. His body was buried at the foot of the orchard" (Chapter 1) — combining structural absence (the death narrated in two short sentences, the visionary removed from the text the moment the diagnosis is delivered) with bathetic understatement (the verb "buried" and the prepositional phrase "at the foot of the orchard" registering the death without ceremony, the structural cause delivered with the lucid economy Orwell's prose requires) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The temporal marker "Three nights later" compresses the gap between vision and execution into a span the reader can hold — and the brevity itself enacts the structural problem: the visionary's absence arrives so quickly after the vision that no accountability framework has been installed to outlive him.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the death not as personal grief but as structural exposure — the foundation laid in Chapter 1 already withdrawn by Chapter 2, the rest of the building therefore at the mercy of whoever subsequently occupies the site.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the recurring totalitarian pattern — the visionary's death as the precondition of the strongman's seizure, the absence of the visionary as the silence the regime fills with its own voice.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the original revolutionary case is honourable and that the original revolutionary visionary is honourable, and that the structural failing — the unspecified accountability mechanism — is the disease that recurs across regimes regardless of the visionary's personal merit.

• Context (AO3): Lenin died on 21 January 1924 after a series of strokes; his Testament, dictated in late 1922 and early 1923, warned against Stalin's accumulation of bureaucratic power, but the document was suppressed by the Politburo for thirty-three years — the gap between Major's address and Major's death is the historical gap between Lenin's diagnosis and Lenin's silence.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell constructs Old Major as the honourable diagnosis whose hamartia is the originating structural cause — Chapter 1 articulating the case, the anthem installing the feeling, the death opening the gap that the pigs occupy.

• Controlling concept: What the address installed as the diagnosis, the anthem installed as collective feeling, the death exposed as structural omission — three stages of the same vision, the consistency confirming Orwell's claim that any revolution that ignores accountability ends as the tyranny it overthrew.

• Author's purpose: Writing as a self-described democratic socialist for a 1945 readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell uses Major's hamartia to insist that the diagnosis must outlive the visionary — that the next vision must specify, in advance, the mechanism by which the leadership it produces will be held to account.

• Ultimate moral: Old Major's enduring force is that the diagnosis he articulates remains, after ten chapters of betrayal, the only accurate description of the regime that has destroyed it — and Orwell's pedagogy is to send the reader back to the address with the analytical equipment to defend the diagnosis from the regime that has captured its language.

C9 — Benjamin

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Benjamin to explore ideas about cynicism and silence in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Benjamin

- how Orwell uses Benjamin to explore ideas about cynicism and silence.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Benjamin is the intellectual who sees the betrayal and refuses to speak — the donkey whose cynicism functions as the regime's quietest enabler, whose belated cry over Boxer's van arrives ten chapters after he could have warned the working class, and whose silence Orwell stages as the structural complement to Squealer's noise. The plan tracks three stages — Chapter 3 the cynic installed, Chapter 7 the witness who will not speak, Chapter 9 the recognition that arrives too late.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The universal political archetype — Benjamin is the intellectual whose refusal to speak is the regime's quietest enabler; the structural foil to Boxer (investment without analysis vs analysis without investment); the cynic Orwell himself wrote against in his political journalism. Land in BP1 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Benjamin: the Biblical Benjamin, the youngest son of Jacob; the donkey whose longevity surveys all the regimes; sometimes read as the older Russian intelligentsia or the disillusioned veteran of past failed politics.

• Foil pairing — Benjamin against Boxer: analysis without investment against investment without analysis; both survive the revolution and only one is destroyed by it.

• Apophasis — saying nothing said with rhetorical weight; Benjamin's "Donkeys live a long time. None of you has ever seen a dead donkey" (Chapter 3) refusing the political analysis his survival positions him to deliver.

• Belated recognition — the gap between Benjamin's literacy (he can read) and his speech (he refuses to use it) finally collapsing at the side of the knacker's van.

• Refusal to read — Benjamin's literacy as the regime's most underused asset.

• Silence as enabling — the structural complement to Squealer's noise.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Russian intelligentsia — the educated, often liberal class of writers, teachers and professionals who had populated the late nineteenth-century debates about the country's future — was systematically excluded from political life during the consolidation of Stalinism across the 1920s and 1930s, many emigrating, many silenced, many executed, and the silent witness Orwell would identify in "Why I Write" (1946) as the species of intellectual whose refusal to speak was the regime's quietest enabler.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had spent his BBC Postscripts years (1940–43) and his subsequent journalism arguing for the writer's responsibility to name what is happening, constructs Benjamin as the intellectual whose literacy is the regime's most underused asset and whose cynicism the regime requires — the donkey whose long survival accumulates the evidence the working class needed and whose refusal to articulate it is the precondition of the working class's destruction.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 3 cynic's maxim "Donkeys live a long time. None of you has ever seen a dead donkey" in which intellectual literacy is converted into political withdrawal, his Chapter 7 silent witnessing of the public executions in which the donkey who could speak refuses to, and his Chapter 9 belated cry of "Fools!" at the side of the knacker's van that arrives ten chapters too late, Orwell presents Benjamin as the intellectual whose refusal to speak is the regime's quietest enabler and whose silence is the structural complement to Squealer's noise.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: the cynic installed (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Benjamin's refusal to commit to either side of the revolutionary argument; his cynic's maxim.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Benjamin not as the revolution's opponent but as its withholder — the donkey who reads the writing on every wall and converts that reading into the cynic's refusal to deploy it, the intellectual literacy he possesses already withheld from the working class who lack it.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys apophasis paired with the negative construction of intellectual withholding — "Donkeys live a long time. None of you has ever seen a dead donkey" (Chapter 3) — combining apophasis (the maxim that says nothing positive about the politics it surveys, the rhetorical structure of refusing-to-speak performed in the speaking) with negative construction ("None of you has ever seen", the syntax of withdrawal that converts long survival into the licence to refuse engagement) inside the same maxim.

• Close analysis: The verb "live" registers Benjamin's structural advantage — the survival the working class lacks — while the closing "never seen a dead donkey" converts that advantage into the cynic's licence, the long-lived intellectual exempting himself from the political contest his survival should oblige him to enter.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the chill of Benjamin's withdrawal — the intellectual who possesses the literacy the regime requires concealed refusing to deploy it, the silence registering as a political position rather than absence.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's structural choice — that the foil to Boxer's investment-without-analysis is Benjamin's analysis-without-investment, and that the regime requires both: the worker who cannot see and the intellectual who refuses to say.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the intellectual's refusal to speak is not neutral but enabling — that the regime requires the cynic's silence as much as it requires the propagandist's noise, and that the literacy Benjamin possesses is the working class's most underused political asset.

• Context (AO3): The Russian intelligentsia in the 1920s and 1930s included many figures who, like Benjamin, survived the regime by withdrawing from the political contest the regime required — and Orwell's own essay "Inside the Whale" (1940) had diagnosed the equivalent withdrawal of the British literary class during the inter-war period, the historical pattern Benjamin condenses into one donkey.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 7: the witness who will not speak (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the public executions on the killing knoll; Benjamin watches with the others; he says nothing.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed Benjamin as the cynic, Chapter 7 stages him as the silent witness — the donkey whose long survival has accumulated the evidence the working class need to recognise the regime present at the public executions and using his presence not to name the operation but to confirm his earlier withdrawal.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys narrative withholding paired with the catalogue of those who do speak — the chapter's catalogued response after the executions includes Boxer's "I do not understand it. I would not have believed that such things could happen on our farm" and Clover's wordless gathering of the smaller animals — combining narrative withholding (Benjamin's absence from the catalogued response, the close-analysis grammar registering the silence the chapter requires the reader to notice) with the catalogue of those who do speak (Boxer and Clover supplying the working-class registers Benjamin refuses to add to) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The negative construction of Benjamin's absence performs the regime's quietest enabling — the intellectual present at the spectacle and contributing nothing to the working-class effort to name it — while the closing of Boxer's "I do not understand it" registers the literacy gap Benjamin's silence has refused to close, the syntax of working-class bewilderment unanswered by the intellectual whose answer the chapter could have provided.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels Benjamin's silence as a political position — the absence of his voice from the catalogued response registering as the species of withdrawal Orwell's later essay "Politics and the English Language" will diagnose.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the structural form of intellectual enabling — that the regime requires the cynic to refuse the analytical service his literacy makes available, and that Benjamin's silence is therefore the regime's quietest instrument.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the intellectual's responsibility is to name the operation when the working class lacks the literacy to do so — and that the cynic's refusal to discharge that responsibility is the structural condition of the regime's continuation.

• Context (AO3): The 1936–38 Moscow show trials saw many of the surviving Russian intelligentsia — those who had not been executed in the purges and had not emigrated — accept the regime's official narrative in print, the silence-or-collaboration choice the historical pattern Orwell condenses into Benjamin's catalogued absence from the response to the killing knoll.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 9: recognition that arrives too late (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Boxer's collapse and the knacker's van; Benjamin finally speaks; the cry of "Fools!"

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 7 had installed Benjamin as the silent witness, Chapter 9 stages the belated recognition — the donkey who would not speak when the speaking could have saved the working class finally speaking ten chapters too late, the cry of "Fools!" registering the literacy that was always present and the political failure of its earlier withholding.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys exclamatory anaphora paired with the rhetorical question of the witness's literacy — "'Fools! Fools!' shouted Benjamin, prancing round them and stamping the earth with his small hoofs. 'Fools! Do you not see what is written on the side of that van?'" (Chapter 9) — combining exclamatory anaphora (the triple "Fools!" registering the urgency the donkey had withheld for nine chapters) with rhetorical question ("Do you not see what is written", the literacy gap finally addressed in the form of a question the gap itself had made impossible to answer) inside the same outburst.

• Close analysis: The exclamation "Fools!" condenses the intellectual's belated recognition into a single noun — the literacy Benjamin always possessed deployed for the first time against the working class he had refused to assist — while the participle "prancing" and "stamping" register the embodied panic that the cynic's earlier maxim had explicitly refused to permit.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the bitterness of the recognition — the literacy that would have saved Boxer deployed only after Boxer's loss, the intellectual's response arriving as grief rather than warning.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the controlling concept's structural form — that the working class's destruction is the precondition of the intellectual's voice, and that the regime requires the intellectual to wait until the working class has been consumed before permitting the analysis the working class needed before the consumption.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the intellectual's refusal to speak is the regime's quietest enabler — that Benjamin's literacy, withheld for nine chapters, would have been the working class's most valuable defence against the rewriting and the appropriation that consumed Boxer, and that the cry of "Fools!" arrives too late because the withholding was the regime's structural condition.

• Context (AO3): Orwell's own essay "Why I Write" (1946) describes the obligation of the writer in totalitarian times as "to make political writing into an art" — the explicit articulation of the responsibility Benjamin refuses, and the historical record of which writers across the 1930s and 1940s discharged the responsibility and which (like Benjamin) withheld it the literary-historical pattern Orwell's donkey condenses into a single character.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell constructs Benjamin as the intellectual whose refusal to speak is the regime's quietest enabler — Chapter 3 the cynic installed, Chapter 7 the silent witness, Chapter 9 the belated recognition that arrives ten chapters too late.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the cynic's licence to withdraw, Chapter 7 confirmed as the witness's silence at the killing knoll, Chapter 9 exposed as the belated cry over Boxer's van — three stages of the same withholding, the intellectual's literacy converted into the regime's structural advantage at every beat.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1945 readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell may be using Benjamin to argue that the intellectual class is responsible for the working class's literacy gap — and that the cynic's silence is therefore not neutral observation but active enabling of the regime the silence makes possible.

• Ultimate moral: Benjamin's enduring force as a fable creation is that the reader, possessing the literacy the working class lacks, is invited to recognise themselves in the donkey — and Orwell's pedagogy is to convert that recognition into the responsibility the donkey refused, the analytical move the citizens of Animal Farm needed ten chapters before they received it.

C10 — Moses the Raven

Question

How does Orwell use the character of Moses the Raven to explore ideas about religion and consolation in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how Orwell presents Moses

- how Orwell uses Moses to explore ideas about religion and consolation.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Moses the Raven is the regime's religious-consolation function externalised into a single bird — the tame raven whose tales of "Sugarcandy Mountain" placate the animals before the revolution, who disappears with Jones when the regime no longer needs him, and who returns under Napoleon to perform the same consolation under new management. The plan tracks three stages — Chapter 2 the original opium, Chapter 5 the disappearance, Chapter 9 the return that confirms the cycle.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — Moses condenses the Russian Orthodox Church (state-suppressed under Lenin from 1917, partially restored under Stalin during the Great Patriotic War 1941–45) AND the universal political archetype of religion as the opium of the masses (Marx's Critique of Hegel's Philosophy of Right, 1844). Land in BP3 or the conclusion.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Moses: the Biblical prophet who promised the Promised Land; here, the priest who promises "Sugarcandy Mountain" in lieu of present justice.

• Allegorical mapping — Moses = the Russian Orthodox Church under the late Tsarist regime + Stalinist concordat (1943).

• Symbolic geography — "Sugarcandy Mountain" the heaven that excuses the present world's suffering.

• Disappearance and return — Moses leaves with Jones (Chapter 5) and returns under Napoleon (Chapter 9), the cyclical structure registering the regime's permanent need for the consolation function.

• Marx allusion — "religion is the opium of the people" (Critique of Hegel's Philosophy of Right, 1844); the diagnosis Orwell's narrative enacts.

• Concordat with the new regime — Napoleon's grant of an "allowance of beer" to Moses (Chapter 9), the regime's open admission that the consolation function has been re-purchased.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Karl Marx wrote in his Critique of Hegel's Philosophy of Right (1844) that "religion is the opium of the people" — the diagnosis that organised religion functioned, under capitalism, as the ideological consolation that excused the working class's present suffering by promising a future heavenly reward — and the Russian Orthodox Church, suppressed by the Bolsheviks after 1917, was partially restored by Stalin during the Great Patriotic War (1941–45) when the regime required the consolation function the Church could still supply.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who described himself in "Why I Write" (1946) as a "democratic socialist" and whose political journalism had repeatedly argued that totalitarianism was a secular religion in form, constructs Moses the Raven as the religious-consolation function externalised into a single bird — the tame raven who survives every regime change because every regime requires the consolation Moses supplies.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 2 "Sugarcandy Mountain" tales told to the animals under Jones, his Chapter 5 disappearance with Jones when the early revolution suppresses the consolation function, and his Chapter 9 return under Napoleon with an officially-sanctioned "allowance of beer", Orwell presents Moses as the religious-consolation function the regime requires — the opium externalised into a bird whose disappearance and return register the regime's structural need for the present world's suffering to be excused.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 2: the original opium (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Moses tells the animals about "Sugarcandy Mountain"; the animals listen; the pigs initially try to discredit him.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Moses not as Jones's accessory but as the regime's structural consolation function — the raven whose tales of "Sugarcandy Mountain" convert the present world's suffering into the promise of future heavenly reward, the diagnostic case for the opium-of-the-masses argument condensed into a single bird.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys allegorical naming paired with symbolic geography — "Mr. Jones's especial pet, was a spy and a tale-bearer, but he was also a clever talker. He claimed to know of the existence of a mysterious country called Sugarcandy Mountain, to which all animals went when they died" (Chapter 2) — combining allegorical naming (the Biblical Moses, the prophet who promised the Promised Land, named into the raven who promises a heavenly equivalent) with symbolic geography ("Sugarcandy Mountain", the comically saccharine heaven that registers the consolation function as escapism from present injustice) inside the same characterisation.

• Close analysis: The compound noun "spy and a tale-bearer" registers Moses's structural double-function — the consolation-supplier doubling as the regime's informant, the syntax exposing the political utility of the religious function to the regime that sponsors it — while the adjective "mysterious" in "mysterious country" exposes the epistemological move the consolation requires, the impossibility of verifying the promise that licenses the believer's acceptance of present injustice.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the comic register of "Sugarcandy Mountain" and feels the diagnostic precision — the consolation function exposed at the level of its absurdity, the saccharine name laughing at itself while the political function operates beneath.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the regime's structural need for the consolation function — that any regime which extracts the working class's present labour requires a narrative of future compensation, and that Moses is the externalisation of that requirement into a single bird.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the religious-consolation function is not a misunderstanding to be corrected but a structural component of the regime — and that the diagnostic move the novella requires is the recognition that the consolation excuses the present injustice rather than relieving it.

• Context (AO3): Karl Marx's Critique of Hegel's Philosophy of Right (1844) diagnosed religion as "the opium of the people" in the context of nineteenth-century European industrial capitalism — the Russian Orthodox Church under the late Tsarist regime was the textbook case of the diagnosis, the historical pattern Orwell condenses into Moses's tales of "Sugarcandy Mountain".

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 5: the disappearance (7 sentences — about 225 words)

Scene focus: Moses disappears with Jones after the rebellion; the pigs initially discourage talk of "Sugarcandy Mountain".

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 2 had installed Moses as the original opium, Chapter 5 stages his disappearance — the raven who served the Jones regime departing with Jones when the new revolution suppresses the consolation function, the structural beat registering the early revolution's recognition that the consolation function and the regime that requires it are political partners rather than independent operators.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys narrative ellipsis paired with the pigs' explicit suppression — "Moses, who was Mr. Jones's especial pet… did no work at all… The animals hated Moses because he told tales and did no work, but some of them believed in Sugarcandy Mountain" (Chapter 2) and the later "the pigs had to argue very hard to persuade them that there was no such place" (Chapter 2 retrospectively) — combining narrative ellipsis (Moses's actual departure passed over in the narrative compression that characterises the immediate post-revolution chapters) with the pigs' explicit suppression (the verbs "argue" and "persuade" registering the early revolution's political work against the consolation function) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The negative construction "that there was no such place" exposes the political work the early revolution requires — the suppression of the consolation function as the precondition of mobilising the working class toward present action — while the verb "argue very hard" registers the difficulty of that suppression, the consolation function's grip on the working class the regime cannot afford to leave in place.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the early revolution's diagnostic clarity — the consolation function correctly identified as the regime's structural partner and explicitly opposed by the new principles the revolution is installing.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural pattern — that the early revolution's anti-consolation position is the moment of the diagnosis's clearest articulation, and that any subsequent return of the consolation function will register as the regime's betrayal of its own founding principles.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the early revolution's clarity about the consolation function is the moment the controlling concept is at its most lucid — and that any subsequent restoration of the consolation will therefore signal the regime's loss of the analytical equipment with which it began.

• Context (AO3): The Bolsheviks after 1917 suppressed the Russian Orthodox Church through the seizure of church property, the closure of seminaries, and the public discrediting of religious doctrine in the Bezbozhnik (Atheist) magazine of the late 1920s and 1930s — the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the pigs' explicit suppression of Moses and the consolation function he supplies.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 9: the return that confirms the cycle (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Moses returns to the farm under Napoleon's regime; the pigs grant him an "allowance of beer"; the tales of "Sugarcandy Mountain" resume.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 5 had installed the disappearance, Chapter 9 stages the return that confirms the cycle — Moses returning to the farm under Napoleon's regime with an officially-sanctioned "allowance of beer", the consolation function the early revolution had explicitly suppressed now openly re-purchased by the new tyranny, the structural beat registering the regime's recognition that the working class's present suffering requires the promise of future heavenly reward.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys cyclical return paired with the open admission of state-religion concordat — "In the middle of the summer Moses the raven suddenly reappeared on the farm, after an absence of several years. He was quite unchanged… he still claimed, in his solemn manner, that there was such a place. 'Up there, comrades,' he would say solemnly, pointing to the sky with his large beak — 'up there, just on the other side of that dark cloud that you can see — there it lies, Sugarcandy Mountain'… The pigs… declared that his stories about Sugarcandy Mountain were lies, and yet allowed him to remain on the farm, not working, with an allowance of a gill of beer a day" (Chapter 9) — combining cyclical return (Moses's "quite unchanged" status registering the regime's structural need for the same consolation function across regime changes) with the open admission of state-religion concordat (the "allowance of a gill of beer a day" registering the regime's purchase of the consolation function the regime publicly denies) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "quite unchanged" registers the consolation function's structural permanence — the bird who served Jones now serving Napoleon with no modification to his message — while the contradictory clause "declared that his stories… were lies, and yet allowed him to remain" exposes the regime's political utility: the lie the regime denies is the lie the regime requires.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the cyclical structure consummate — the consolation function the early revolution had suppressed now openly restored, the regime's progression from anti-consolation to pro-consolation registering as the betrayal of the founding diagnosis.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the controlling concept's terminal claim — that the unaccountable regime reproduces every structural component of the regime it overthrew, including the consolation function the original diagnosis explicitly opposed.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the regime's restoration of the consolation function is the precondition of its continuing extraction — that the working class whose present suffering is excused by the promise of heavenly reward is the working class that will continue to supply the labour the regime requires.

• Context (AO3): Stalin restored partial recognition of the Russian Orthodox Church during the Great Patriotic War (1941–45) — the September 1943 concordat permitted the election of a Patriarch, the reopening of churches, the publication of religious literature in return for the Church's public support of the war effort — the historical pattern Orwell condenses into Napoleon's "allowance of a gill of beer a day" to the returned Moses.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell uses Moses the Raven to dramatise the regime's structural need for the religious-consolation function — Chapter 2 the original opium, Chapter 5 the disappearance during the early revolution's diagnostic clarity, Chapter 9 the return that confirms the cycle.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 2 installed as Jones's consolation function, Chapter 5 explicitly suppressed during the revolution's lucid moment, Chapter 9 openly restored under Napoleon with state sponsorship — three stages of the same operation, the consolation function the regime requires regardless of the vocabulary in which the regime presents itself.

• Author's purpose: Writing as a self-described democratic socialist for a 1945 readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell may be using Moses to argue that the unaccountable regime reproduces every structural component of the regime it overthrew — including the consolation function the original diagnosis explicitly opposed.

• Ultimate moral: Moses's enduring force as a fable creation is that his return under Napoleon registers as the cycle's terminal proof — and Orwell's pedagogy is to train the reader to recognise that any regime which restores the consolation function it had previously suppressed has, by that act, completed its reproduction of the regime it once overthrew.

Top 10 Theme Questions

Most likely AQA Modern Text theme questions, ordered by examiner-prediction confidence. Identical scaffolding to the character set — every plan is yours to memorise, redraft, and write against.

T1 — Power and Corruption

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about power and corruption in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what different characters do

- how Orwell presents ideas about power and corruption.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Power and corruption are the novella's central political diagnosis — installed by the milk-and-apples appropriation within hours of the revolution, escalated through the dogs and the rewriting of the commandments, and consummated in the closing dinner where the pigs and humans become indistinguishable. The plan tracks three stages — the first appropriation, the institutional seizure, the final indistinguishability.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — every stage of the corruption arc has a precise Soviet-historical anchor (the nomenklatura under Lenin; the abolition of factions at the Tenth Party Congress 1921; the Tehran Conference 1943) AND a universal political archetype (the unaccountable leadership; the closure of debate; the alliance with former enemies). Land in BP3 or the conclusion.

Key technique terms:

• Cyclical structure — Manor Farm renamed Animal Farm renamed Manor Farm; the form is the corruption.

• Symbolic appropriation — milk and apples (Chapter 3); the farmhouse beds (Chapter 6); the whisky (Chapters 8–10).

• Auditory escalation — the "baying" of the dogs; the bleating of the sheep; the singing replaced by silence.

• Allegorical compression — the Five-Year Plans condensed into the windmill; the Great Terror condensed into the public executions.

• Visual climax — the pigs walking on two legs; the whip in the trotter (Chapter 10).

• Foil pairing — the principles announced (Chapter 2) against the principles rewritten (Chapter 10).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): "Power tends to corrupt, and absolute power corrupts absolutely" — Lord Acton wrote those words in a private letter in April 1887, and Orwell, who first encountered the formulation as a schoolboy, used the diagnosis as the spine of his political writing from Homage to Catalonia (1938) through Animal Farm (1945) to Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949).

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell wrote Animal Farm between November 1943 and February 1944 while the Soviet Union, then Britain's wartime ally, was being celebrated in British public discourse as a model of revolutionary success — and the novella was rejected by Faber, Gollancz and Cape on those grounds before Secker & Warburg published it in August 1945, the timing of the publication ensuring the diagnosis arrived as the Cold War was opening rather than as the Second World War was closing.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 3 milk-and-apples appropriation in which the corruption begins within hours of the revolution, the Chapter 5–7 institutional seizure in which the dogs replace debate and the commandments are rewritten to license every transgression, and the Chapter 10 closing dinner in which the pigs and humans become indistinguishable, Orwell dramatises power and corruption as the structural disease that recurs across regimes — power without accountability the bacillus, the rewriting of the commandments the symptom, the closing image of pig and man the prognosis.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: first appropriation (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the pigs take the milk and apples for themselves within hours of the revolution; Squealer arrives to explain.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs power's corruption not as the revolution's gradual decay but as its immediate consequence — the milk-and-apples appropriation occurring within hours of the revolution itself, the corruption arriving so early that the principle of the revolution and the betrayal of the principle share the same chapter.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys narrative compression inside ironic juxtaposition — "It had also been agreed that nobody else must come near it. So the milk was given to the pigs" (Chapter 3) — combining narrative compression (the appropriation arriving in a single short sentence, the brevity itself enacting the velocity of the corruption) with ironic juxtaposition (the verb "agreed" in the previous clause, the agreement engineered by the pigs whose appropriation the agreement licenses) inside the same paragraph.

• Close analysis: The passive "was given" erases agency — the milk given to no specified giver — while the modal "must" in "must come near it" registers the pre-emptive prohibition that protects the appropriation, the syntax confirming that the corruption requires its own protective vocabulary from the moment of its first occurrence.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the velocity of the betrayal — the corruption arriving so early that the reader is still recovering from the revolution itself — recognising that the gap between the principle and the practice is not the slow erosion of years but the immediate consequence of the revolution's failure to specify accountability.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise structural claim — that the milk and apples episode is not a marginal moment but the prototype of every subsequent appropriation, the corruption a single bacillus seeded in the revolution's first hours and replicated across the rest of the chapters.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the revolution's failure to specify accountability mechanisms is the originating cause — that the pigs would not have taken the milk and apples if the institutional structures had been in place to prevent it, and that the corruption is therefore not personal vice but the structural consequence of the gap Old Major's vision left.
