• Context (AO3): The Bolshevik leadership, within months of the October 1917 revolution, was already consolidating its own privileges — the nomenklatura system, established under Lenin and entrenched under Stalin, gave Party officials access to special shops, dachas and medical care that the general population did not — and the milk and apples episode is the institutional pattern condensed into a single morning.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 5–7: institutional seizure (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the dogs drive Snowball off the farm; the Sunday meetings are abolished; the commandments are rewritten.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed the corruption as immediate appropriation, Chapters 5–7 stage its institutionalisation — the dogs replacing debate, the Sunday meetings abolished, the commandments rewritten one by one — the corruption now no longer the regime's behaviour but the regime's permanent constitution.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys symbolic action paired with passive announcement — "He announced that from now on the Sunday-morning Meetings would come to an end… All questions relating to the working of the farm would be settled by a special committee of pigs, presided over by himself" (Chapter 5) — combining symbolic action (the abolition of the Sunday meetings ending the only forum in which the principles could be defended through discussion) with passive announcement (the bureaucratic register "would come to an end", the agentless grammar disguising the seizure as administrative reform) inside the same announcement.

• Close analysis: The phrase "presided over by himself" exposes the seizure precisely because it states what the announcement was engineered to conceal — Napoleon's personal authority installed inside the language of collective governance, the close-analysis grammar registering the corruption that the announcement's vocabulary is performing.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the velocity of the institutional seizure — the corruption no longer requiring justification because the institution that would require justification has just been abolished.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the structural form of the corruption is the abolition of the institutions through which it could be challenged — that the totalitarian regime's first administrative move is to remove the procedural mechanisms that would allow accountability to operate.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that power's corruption is not maintained through ongoing terror alone but through the abolition of the institutions of accountability — that the regime requires not only the dogs but the closure of the meetings, not only the executions but the rewriting of the commandments, and that each of these moves is the corruption made structural rather than personal.

• Context (AO3): The Soviet Union's transition from revolutionary councils (soviets) to one-party rule was effectively complete by 1921 with the banning of factions within the Communist Party at the Tenth Party Congress; by the late 1920s the Central Committee was a rubber stamp for Stalin's personal decisions, and the abolition of the Sunday meetings in Animal Farm condenses the institutional pattern into a single chapter.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 10: indistinguishability (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the pigs walk on two legs; the closing dinner with Pilkington; the final image of pig and man.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–7 had institutionalised the corruption, Chapter 10 consummates it — the pigs walking on two legs, the closing dinner with Pilkington enacting the alliance with the very humans the revolution was meant to overthrow, and the final image of pig and man indistinguishable announcing that the corruption's ultimate achievement is the regime's indistinguishability from the regime it replaced.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys syntactic mirroring inside cyclical closure — "The creatures outside looked from pig to man, and from man to pig, and from pig to man again; but already it was impossible to say which was which" (Chapter 10) — combining syntactic mirroring (the parallel construction "from pig to man, and from man to pig, and from pig to man again" enacting perceptual confusion within the sentence rhythm itself, the categories dissolving in the act of reading) with cyclical closure (the ending returning to the dynamic the revolution was launched to overthrow, the form announcing that the cycle has completed) inside the same final sentence.

• Close analysis: The adverb "already" compresses the entire ten-chapter trajectory into inevitability — the corruption named as having occurred in the past, before the watching animals could have named it — while the passive "it was impossible" strips the watching animals of agency, the syntax enacting the precise condition the controlling concept names: the corruption complete, the watching impossible to convert into action.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the closing image as a permanent residue — the indistinguishability lodging in the reader's mind beyond the text, the warning carried into every subsequent regime the reader will read about.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise pedagogical move — the corruption named not as accident but as the inevitable consequence of unaccountable power, the closing image therefore not the failure of the revolution but the warning that the operation must be recognised before the next regime reproduces it.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the corruption's most lethal achievement is the indistinguishability — that the regime which has captured the revolution's vocabulary is the regime that the citizens of the subsequent generation cannot resist because they cannot name it as different from what it replaced.

• Context (AO3): The November 1943 Tehran Conference saw Stalin received as co-architect of the post-war order alongside Churchill and Roosevelt; by the August 1945 publication of Animal Farm, Soviet leaders had become indistinguishable from the Tsarist aristocracy in their use of palaces, secret police and personal cult — Orwell's closing image is the historical record condensed into a single sentence.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell anatomises power and corruption as the structural disease that recurs across regimes — Chapter 3 installing the first appropriation, Chapters 5–7 institutionalising the seizure, Chapter 10 consummating the indistinguishability.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the immediate consequence of unaccountable power, Chapters 5–7 institutionalised through the abolition of the meetings and the rewriting of the commandments, Chapter 10 consummated through the closing image — three stages of the same diagnosis, the consistency confirming that the corruption is not personal vice but the inevitable consequence of any leadership not subject to accountability.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a readership about to enter the Cold War, Orwell uses the corruption arc to argue that anti-Stalinism is not anti-socialism — that the diagnosis must be applied to every leadership in every era, regardless of the vocabulary in which the leadership presents itself.

• Ultimate moral: Power's corruption is the bacillus the revolution must, in advance of seizing power, specify the prophylactic against — and the reader who closes the novella with the closing image lodged in memory has been taught the analytical move that, applied to the next leadership, may close the gap Old Major's vision did not.

T2 — Propaganda and the Manipulation of Language

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about propaganda and the manipulation of language in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how language is used by different characters

- how Orwell presents ideas about propaganda and the manipulation of language.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Propaganda and language are the novella's primary instrument of corruption — the rewriting of the commandments, the simplification of the slogans, the replacement of the anthem, the euphemism that conceals the slaughter. The plan tracks three stages — the simplification (Chapter 3), the rewriting (Chapters 6–8), the cult (Chapters 8–10).

Pedagogical depth to thread. The rewriting-history mechanism — Orwell's argument in "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946) that vague, abstract, euphemistic prose is the linguistic instrument of totalitarianism is the diagnosis the novella dramatises. Land in BP2 or the conclusion.

Key technique terms:

• Simplification of complex truth — "Four legs good, two legs bad" (Chapter 3) reducing the Seven Commandments to a single bleat.

• Pluperfect rewriting — "had seemed", "had been"; the grammatical instrument of historical falsification.

• Euphemism — "readjustment", "the necessary surgical operation", the lie about the veterinary surgeon.

• Sycophantic register — Squealer's "Surely, comrades, you would not want Jones back?" converting dissent into nostalgia for tyranny.

• Replacement anthem — "Beasts of England" (Chapter 1) abolished in Chapter 7 and replaced by "Comrade Napoleon" in Chapter 8.

• Choric repetition — the sheep's bleating drowning out dissent at every critical political moment.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Orwell published "Politics and the English Language" in Horizon magazine in April 1946 — the year after Animal Farm — and the essay's central argument was that the slovenly abstract prose of mid-twentieth-century political writing was the linguistic instrument by which totalitarianism converted clear thought into vague reflex; the diagnosis the essay analyses is the operation the novella has already dramatised.

• Building sentence (historical context): Pravda, the Soviet Communist Party's official newspaper founded in 1912 and Stalinised after 1924, had by the mid-1930s perfected the rhetorical operations Squealer deploys — the rewriting of the historical record, the manufacture of confessions for the Moscow show trials, the conversion of every famine and purge into evidence of revolutionary success.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 3 simplification of the Seven Commandments into the sheep's "Four legs good, two legs bad", the Chapters 6–8 rewriting of the commandments to license every transgression of the original principles, and the Chapters 8–10 personality cult and replacement anthem that complete the regime's linguistic seizure of the revolution's vocabulary, Orwell presents propaganda and the manipulation of language as the totalitarian regime's most economical instrument — the rhetorical operation that converts every betrayal into the language of continuity and ensures the gap between regime action and regime vocabulary remains permanently closed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: simplification (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the Seven Commandments are simplified for the sheep to "Four legs good, two legs bad".

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs the regime's linguistic operation not as deception alone but as simplification — the conversion of the Seven Commandments into the sheep's single bleat, the political analysis of Animalism reduced to a reflex maxim that drowns out dissent at every subsequent critical moment.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys choric repetition inside reductive paraphrase — "the sheep developed a great liking for this maxim, and often as they lay in the field they would all start bleating 'Four legs good, two legs bad! Four legs good, two legs bad!' and keep it up for hours on end, never growing tired of it" (Chapter 3) — combining choric repetition (the bleating that drowns dissent at every critical moment, the chorus inverted from moral commentary into propaganda) with reductive paraphrase (the seven commandments compressed into a four-word bleat, the simplification eliminating the cognitive space in which the original principles could be analysed) inside the same paragraph.

• Close analysis: The verb "never growing tired" registers the bleating as the propagandised mass's permanent condition — the chorus replaceable by no other utterance — while the temporal marker "for hours on end" signals the displacement of all alternative speech, the bleating consuming the silence in which dissent would otherwise occur.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the bleating as the auditory form of the controlling concept's first symptom — the simplification of complex political truth into a reflex incapable of distinguishing the regime from the principles the regime has betrayed.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the propaganda's first operation is not the explicit lie but the prior simplification — the masses must be taught the reflex before the lie can be installed inside it.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the propagandist's first job is not to deceive but to simplify — that vague, abstract, reflex-ready language is the precondition of every subsequent lie, and that the citizens of any regime must defend complex thought as the prophylactic against the propaganda the regime requires.

• Context (AO3): Orwell's essay "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), written the year after Animal Farm, anatomises the same operation — slovenly abstract prose as the linguistic instrument of totalitarianism — and identifies the simplification of complex truth as the regime's most economical instrument of control.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 6–8: rewriting (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the commandments are rewritten to add "with sheets" (Chapter 6); "to excess" (Chapter 8); "without cause" (Chapter 8).

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed the simplification, Chapters 6–8 stage the rewriting — the commandments altered one by one to license every transgression of the principles, the propaganda apparatus now no longer simplifying the principles but actively converting them into their opposites while the wall on which they are painted appears unchanged.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the rewriting of the commandments paired with the animals' silent bewilderment — the commandment "No animal shall sleep in a bed" (Chapter 2) becomes "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets" (Chapter 6); "No animal shall drink alcohol" becomes "No animal shall drink alcohol to excess" (Chapter 8); "No animal shall kill any other animal" becomes "No animal shall kill any other animal without cause" (Chapter 8) — combining the formal device of the rewritten commandment (each addition consisting of two or three words that license the transgression while preserving the appearance of the original principle) with the animals' silent bewilderment (Clover's recurring "surely it had been different in the old days", the gap between memory and text the technique is engineered to widen).

• Close analysis: The qualifier "with sheets" performs the rewriting precisely because it consists of two words — the smallest possible alteration that nevertheless permits the entire transgression — while the prepositional phrases "to excess" and "without cause" perform the same operation, the close-analysis grammar exposing that the propaganda requires only the smallest grammatical addition to convert any principle into its opposite.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader sees the rewriting in real time — the commandments altered between chapters in the reader's view, the animals' inability to register the change generating the same horror the simplification began to install.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the propaganda apparatus is not merely defending the regime against criticism but actively converting the principles by which the regime could be criticised into the principles by which the regime can defend itself — the language of accountability captured by the language of the regime.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the rewriting of the commandments is the central instrument of the regime — more central than the dogs, more central than the executions, more central than the appropriation — because the rewriting eliminates the standard against which the regime could be measured, and a regime that has captured the standard cannot be held to it.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record; the Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) (1938) rewrote the entire revolutionary history to align with Stalin's preferred narrative — the rewriting of the commandments in Chapters 6–8 condenses the textual operation Stalinism perfected into the wall of the big barn.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 8–10: cult and replacement anthem (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: "Beasts of England" abolished in Chapter 7 and replaced by Minimus's "Comrade Napoleon" in Chapter 8; Napoleon's accumulating titles; the final commandment.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 6–8 had rewritten the commandments, Chapters 8–10 stage the language's final consolidation — the original revolutionary anthem abolished and replaced by the personality cult, Napoleon's titles inflating beyond rational evaluation, and the final commandment "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" completing the regime's linguistic capture of the revolution's founding principle.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys oxymoron inside cumulative rewriting — the final commandment in Chapter 10 — "ALL ANIMALS ARE EQUAL BUT SOME ANIMALS ARE MORE EQUAL THAN OTHERS" — combining oxymoron (the contradictory yoking of "equal" and "more equal", the comparative rendering the absolute meaningless within the same sentence) with cumulative rewriting (the entire seven commandments collapsed into a single line, the conclusion of the operation that began in Chapter 3 with the simplification and continued in Chapters 6–8 with the additions).

• Close analysis: The capitalised typography in the original text registers the regime's appropriation of public space — the commandment now the only text on the wall — while the comparative "more equal" exposes the propaganda's most audacious move: the conversion of the founding principle of equality into the licence for the inequality the regime has produced, the syntax enacting the inversion it announces.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the closure of the linguistic operation — the rewriting and the simplification and the cult having consumed every prior register, the wall now displaying the single line that licenses everything the regime has done.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the language of the revolution has been completely captured — that there is no longer a vocabulary in which the regime could be criticised, because the regime has appropriated even the words by which the criticism could be articulated.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the totalitarian regime's most lethal achievement is not the coup but the linguistic capture — that the regime which holds the language holds the future, and that the citizens whose language has been captured cannot resist a regime they cannot name.

• Context (AO3): Stalin's official titles by the late 1930s included "Brilliant Genius of Humanity", "Father of Nations", "Gardener of Human Happiness" — the cataloguing satire of Napoleon's titles in Chapter 8 is the historical pattern condensed into one paragraph, and the slogan "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" condenses the entire Stalinist linguistic operation into a single sentence.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell dramatises propaganda and the manipulation of language as the totalitarian regime's most economical instrument — Chapter 3 installing the simplification, Chapters 6–8 escalating into the rewriting, Chapters 8–10 consummating in the cult and the final oxymoron.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the simplification of complex truth, Chapters 6–8 escalated as the rewriting of the principles, Chapters 8–10 completed as the linguistic capture of the revolution's vocabulary — three stages of the same operation, the language the first thing the new tyranny corrupts to disguise what it is doing.

• Author's purpose: Writing in the same year as "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), Orwell uses the novella to dramatise, at the level of fictional incident, the linguistic operation the essay would diagnose at the level of analysis — the citizens' defence of clear thought as the prophylactic against the regime that requires vague abstract prose.

• Ultimate moral: The language is the future the regime requires — and the reader who closes the novella with the rewritten commandments lodged in memory has been trained to recognise the operation when it appears in the next regime's vocabulary, which is the analytical move the essay names as the citizen's defence.

T3 — Equality

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about equality in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Orwell presents ideas about equality.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Equality is the principle the revolution installs and the principle the revolution betrays — codified in Chapter 2 as the seventh commandment "All animals are equal", defended through the literacy gap the regime requires, and rewritten in Chapter 10 as the founding oxymoron "some animals are more equal than others". The plan tracks three stages — equality articulated as principle, equality undermined by literacy gap, equality consummated as inverted slogan.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — every stage of the equality arc has a precise Soviet-historical anchor (the 1918 Constitution; the 1936 Constitution; the Short Course of 1938 monopolising the historical record) AND a universal political archetype (the principle the regime codifies; the literacy the regime withholds; the slogan the regime inverts). Land in BP2 or the conclusion.

Key technique terms:

• Codification — the conversion of oral vision into public text; the Seven Commandments painted on the barn wall.

• Symbolic naming — "All animals are equal" as the seventh and final commandment.

• Literacy gap — Clover's "only the first letters"; Boxer's "A, B, C, D"; the sheep's bleating displacing reading.

• Final oxymoron — "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" (Chapter 10) — the founding principle and its inversion in the same sentence.

• Cyclical structure — Manor Farm renamed Animal Farm renamed Manor Farm; the form is the inequality.

• Visual climax — the pigs and humans dining together at the closing dinner.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The 1918 Constitution of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic codified universal suffrage, the abolition of class distinctions, and the principle of "All Power to the Soviets" — yet by the early 1920s the soviets had been effectively replaced by Communist Party committees, and Stalin's 1936 Constitution preserved the rhetoric of Soviet democracy (universal suffrage, civil liberties, freedom of speech) while installing the centralised personal authority of the General Secretary, the gap between codified equality and lived inequality the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the barn wall.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who described himself as a "democratic socialist" in "Why I Write" (1946), was therefore not writing a critique of the principle of equality but a warning that the principle would be reproduced as inequality by any leadership that did not specify, in advance, the accountability mechanisms by which the new hierarchy would be prevented.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 2 codification of "All animals are equal" as the seventh and final commandment, the Chapters 3–8 literacy gap through which the rewriting of the commandments operates against the masses who cannot read them, and the Chapter 10 final oxymoron "some animals are more equal than others" that consummates the regime's inversion of the founding principle, Orwell presents equality as the principle the revolution installs and the principle the regime betrays — the distance between the codification and the inversion the structural arc the novella has been measuring for ten chapters.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 2: equality codified as principle (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the Seven Commandments are painted on the barn wall; "All animals are equal" is the seventh and last.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs equality not as the revolution's diffuse aspiration but as its codified principle — the seventh and final commandment painted on the public barn wall, the absolute "All animals are equal" the foundation against which every subsequent inequality will be measured.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys codification paired with visual permanence — "They had been painted on the tarred wall in great white letters that could be read thirty yards away" and the closing "7. All animals are equal" (Chapter 2) — combining codification (the seven commandments converting Animalism from oral vision into public text, the institutional move the revolution requires to outlive the visionary) with visual permanence (the "great white letters" and the spatial measure "thirty yards away" registering the principles as visible to every animal at every moment, the public visibility the procedural form of accountability the principles require) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The absolute "All" in "All animals are equal" admits no exception — the universal quantifier engineered to make any subsequent inequality immediately recognisable as betrayal — while the seventh-place ordinal positions equality as the principle that gathers and confirms the previous six, the syntax of the commandment-sequence registering equality as the keystone the regime must dismantle before any other transgression can be licensed.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the institutional achievement of the codification — the principle installed in public text, visible to every animal, the procedural form of accountability that the gap Old Major's vision left required.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural keystone — that "All animals are equal" is not one commandment among seven but the principle on which the other six depend, and that the regime's eventual rewriting of this commandment is therefore the structural completion of the inversion the previous rewrites have been preparing.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the principle of equality is honourable and that its codification is the necessary institutional move — but that codification without the literacy required to defend the codified principle is the precondition of the rewriting the regime will subsequently perform.

• Context (AO3): The 1918 Constitution of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic codified universal suffrage, the abolition of class distinctions, and the principle of "All Power to the Soviets" — yet by the early 1920s the soviets had been effectively replaced by Communist Party committees, the gap between codified equality and institutional reality the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the barn wall.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 3–8: equality undermined by literacy gap (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Clover's "only the first letters"; the sheep's bleating; the commandments rewritten without the masses' protest.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 2 had codified equality, Chapters 3–8 stage its slow undermining — the literacy gap the regime requires permitting the commandments to be rewritten one by one while the masses lack the cognitive equipment to defend them, the principle of equality progressively converted into its opposite without the masses noticing the conversion.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the literacy hierarchy paired with the silent bewilderment of the working class — "None of the other animals on the farm could get further than the letter A. It was also found that the stupider animals, such as the sheep, hens, and ducks, were unable to learn the Seven Commandments by heart" (Chapter 3) and Clover's recurring "surely there had been no clause" (Chapters 6–8) — combining the literacy hierarchy (the catalogued literacy levels of pigs, dogs, Boxer, Mollie, sheep registering the structural inequality the regime requires) with the silent bewilderment of the working class (Clover's softened "surely" registering the cognitive defeat the literacy gap engineers) inside the same arc.

• Close analysis: The negative construction "None… could get further than the letter A" registers the literacy ceiling as a structural condition rather than a personal limitation — the masses' incapacity engineered by the regime's failure to distribute the literacy the principles require — while Clover's "surely" exposes the regime's victory: the working-class memory softened into concession the moment the public text contradicts it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the structural fragility of the codified principle — equality installed at the level of text but withheld at the level of the literacy required to defend it, the public visibility of the commandments inversely proportional to the working-class capacity to read them.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the regime's most lethal instrument — that the literacy gap is not a passive deficit but an active operation, the regime's control of who can read which texts the precondition of the rewriting that follows.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the principle of equality requires not only its public articulation but the cognitive equipment by which the masses can defend it — and that any revolution that codifies the principles without distributing the literacy is the revolution whose principles can be rewritten without the masses noticing.

• Context (AO3): Soviet literacy campaigns from the 1920s onward did dramatically increase basic literacy, but the Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) (1938) effectively monopolised the historical record in a single text taught across the entire educational system — the literacy that the revolution distributed was, by the late 1930s, literacy in the regime's preferred narrative rather than the principles the revolution had installed.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapter 10: equality consummated as inverted slogan (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the wall now displays only the single rewritten commandment; the closing dinner; the indistinguishability of pig and man.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 3–8 had undermined equality through the literacy gap, Chapter 10 stages the consummation — the seven commandments collapsed into a single oxymoronic line, the principle of "All animals are equal" converted into the licence for the regime's hierarchy, the founding principle and its inversion now occupying the same sentence.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys oxymoron inside cumulative rewriting — "There was nothing there now except a single Commandment. It ran: ALL ANIMALS ARE EQUAL BUT SOME ANIMALS ARE MORE EQUAL THAN OTHERS" (Chapter 10) — combining oxymoron (the contradictory yoking of "equal" and "more equal", the comparative rendering the absolute meaningless within the same sentence) with cumulative rewriting (the seven commandments collapsed into a single line, the conclusion of the operation that began with the rewriting of "No animal shall sleep in a bed" into "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets" in Chapter 6) inside the wall's final inscription.

• Close analysis: The comparative "more equal" exposes the regime's most audacious move precisely because the comparative cannot grammatically modify an absolute — "equal" admits no degrees — and the impossibility of the formulation registers, at the level of grammar, the impossibility of the regime the formulation licenses, the capitalised typography enacting the regime's appropriation of the public space where the original principles were once installed.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the collapse of the codification as the structural form of the controlling concept's claim — the principle of equality consumed by the regime that was installed to defend it, the wall now the regime's billboard rather than the revolution's constitution.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the inversion is more lethal than the abolition — that the regime which has captured the founding principle's vocabulary is the regime that the citizens of the subsequent generation cannot resist because the language by which they could articulate the resistance is now the regime's permitted formulation.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the regime's most lethal achievement is the inversion of the founding principle rather than its destruction — that "some animals are more equal than others" is the regime's announcement that the citizens of the regime no longer have a standard against which to measure it because the standard is now the regime's preferred fiction.

• Context (AO3): Stalin's 1936 Constitution preserved the rhetoric of Soviet democracy — universal suffrage, civil liberties, freedom of speech — while installing the centralised personal authority of the General Secretary; the gap between the codified Constitution and the Great Terror operating beneath it is the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the single line on the wall in Chapter 10.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell anatomises equality as the principle the revolution installs and the principle the regime betrays — Chapter 2 codifying "All animals are equal", Chapters 3–8 undermining the principle through the literacy gap, Chapter 10 consummating the inversion as "some animals are more equal than others".

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 2 codified as the seventh-place keystone, Chapters 3–8 hollowed out through the literacy gap the regime requires, Chapter 10 consummated as the oxymoronic licence for the regime's hierarchy — three stages of the same operation, the principle of equality progressively converted into its opposite without the institutional design required to defend it.

• Author's purpose: Writing as a self-described democratic socialist ("Why I Write", 1946) for a 1945 readership about to elect Attlee's Labour government on a platform of welfare and nationalisation, Orwell uses the equality arc to insist that the principle is honourable and that the institutional design must be specific enough to defend it against the leadership the principle produces.

• Ultimate moral: The reader who closes the novella with the inverted slogan lodged in memory has been trained to demand of every revolution the accountability mechanism Old Major's vision did not specify — the analytical move that protects every subsequent codification of equality from the new Manor Farm.

T4 — Rewriting History

Question

How does Orwell present the rewriting of history in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- how the past is rewritten across the novella

- how Orwell uses the rewriting of history to convey his ideas about the regime.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. The rewriting of history is the regime's most economical instrument — Snowball's role at the Battle of the Cowshed converted into Jones's secret agency, the Seven Commandments altered one by one to license every transgression, and Napoleon installed as "Comrade Napoleon" in the founding-revolutionary slot. The plan tracks three stages — Snowball erased, the commandments rewritten, the cult installed.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The rewriting-history mechanism — Orwell's argument that totalitarianism's signature move is the capture of the past. Land in the conclusion at minimum.

Key technique terms:

• Pluperfect rewriting — "had seemed", "had been" — the grammatical instrument of historical falsification.

• Anaphora — Squealer's repeated "comrades" hiding the rewriting of the record.

• Allegorical mapping — the rewriting of the commandments = the Stalinist rewriting of the historical record (Trotsky removed from photographs; the Short Course monopolising the curriculum).

• Smallest grammatical alteration — "with sheets", "to excess", "without cause"; two- or three-word additions licensing the entire transgression.

• Replacement anthem — "Beasts of England" abolished in Chapter 7 and replaced by "Comrade Napoleon" in Chapter 8.

• Symbolic mural — Napoleon installed in Snowball's place at the Battle of the Cowshed "in a full gallop".

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) was published in 1938 under Stalin's editorial supervision and rewrote the entire history of the Russian Revolution to align with the regime's preferred narrative — Trotsky erased, Stalin's role inflated, the Old Bolsheviks executed in the show trials retroactively converted into traitors — and the textbook was taught as the official history across every educational level of the Soviet Union for the next two decades.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had watched Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks be retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record (the practice meticulously documented by David King in The Commissar Vanishes, 1997), constructs the rewriting of Animal Farm's history as the textual form of the visual erasure — the regime's most lethal instrument because it removes the standard against which the regime could be measured.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 5 erasure of Snowball through Squealer's pluperfect rewriting of the Battle of the Cowshed, the Chapters 6–8 rewriting of the Seven Commandments to license every transgression of the original principles, and the Chapters 8–9 installation of the personality cult through the replacement anthem "Comrade Napoleon" and the Cowshed mural placing Napoleon "in the forefront", Orwell presents the rewriting of history as the regime's structural instrument — the operation that captures the past so that the present can no longer be measured against it.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 5: Snowball erased (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Squealer rewrites Snowball's role at the Battle of the Cowshed; the windmill "had seemed" to be opposed by Napoleon.

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs the rewriting of history not as the regime's accessory but as its earliest operation — Snowball's expulsion in Chapter 5 immediately followed by Squealer's rewriting of Snowball's record, the visionary erased physically before being erased historically.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys pluperfect rewriting inside bureaucratic adverb — Squealer announces that Snowball "had seemed to oppose" the windmill "simply as a manoeuvre to get rid of Snowball" and that "the windmill was, in fact, Napoleon's own creation" (Chapter 5, retrospectively) — combining pluperfect rewriting (the grammatical instrument that converts what was into what had been, the tense rearranging chronology so that opposition becomes foresight) with the bureaucratic adverbs "in fact" and "simply" (the registers of false transparency that announce manipulation as plain truth) inside the same retrospective.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "in fact" performs the totalitarian register precisely because it admits no evidence — the reader, who watched Napoleon urinate on the plans in real time, experiences the gap between remembered reality and official narrative as a physical discomfort that mirrors the animals' own confusion.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels cognitive vertigo — the past the previous chapter installed now overwritten by the chapter the reader is currently inside — the technique enacting the operation it is describing.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that historical falsification is not a propaganda accessory but the regime's central instrument — the past must be rewritten because the present can only be defended if the alternative is erased.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the visionary's posthumous reputation is the regime's most dangerous remaining threat — and that the regime's instrument of choice is not the killing of the visionary but the rewriting of the visionary into traitor, so that even those who remember are taught to doubt their own memory.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record — David King's The Commissar Vanishes (1997) documents the pattern in detail — and Squealer's pluperfect rewriting of Snowball is the textual form of that visual erasure.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 6–8: the commandments rewritten (7 sentences — about 240 words)

Scene focus: "No animal shall sleep in a bed" becomes "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets" in Chapter 6; "No animal shall drink alcohol" becomes "No animal shall drink alcohol to excess" in Chapter 8; "No animal shall kill any other animal" becomes "No animal shall kill any other animal without cause" in Chapter 8.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 5 had erased Snowball, Chapters 6–8 rewrite the principles — the commandments altered one by one to license every transgression, the wall on which they are painted appearing unchanged while the principles painted on it are progressively converted into their opposites.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the smallest grammatical alteration paired with the animals' silent bewilderment — the commandment "No animal shall sleep in a bed" (Chapter 2) becomes "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets" (Chapter 6), and Clover's response — "surely there had been no clause in the Fourth Commandment mentioning sheets" (Chapter 6, paraphrased) — combining the smallest grammatical alteration (the addition of "with sheets" consisting of two words, the smallest possible alteration that nevertheless permits the entire transgression) with the animals' silent bewilderment (the modal "surely", the doubt softened into a concession rather than a confident accusation, the regime's victory the production of doubt rather than the elimination of memory) inside the same scene.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "with sheets" performs the rewriting precisely because it consists of two words — the smallest possible alteration that nevertheless permits the entire transgression — while the modal "surely" in Clover's response registers the technique's efficacy: the doubt arrives as a softened concession, the syntax of the doubt itself the regime's evidence that the rewriting has worked.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader sees the rewriting in real time — the commandments altered between chapters in the reader's view, Clover's softened "surely" registering the cognitive distance that the rewriting has produced, the reader's discomfort mirroring her own.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the rewriting is engineered to operate inside the gap between memory and confidence — that the working class remembers but cannot confidently assert, and that the regime requires the softened "surely" rather than the confident accusation, the regime's victory the production of doubt rather than the elimination of memory.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the regime's most lethal instrument is the rewriting of the standard rather than the breach of the standard — that a regime which has captured the standard cannot be held to it, and that the citizens' defence of the principles therefore requires the literacy and the confidence to assert the original text against the rewritten one.

• Context (AO3): Stalinist photographs of the early Bolsheviks were retouched across the 1930s to remove Trotsky, Kamenev and Zinoviev from the historical record; the Short Course on the History of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) (1938) rewrote the entire revolutionary history to align with Stalin's preferred narrative — the rewriting of the commandments in Chapters 6–8 condenses the textual operation Stalinism perfected into the wall of the big barn.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 8–9: the cult installed (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: "Beasts of England" abolished in Chapter 7 and replaced by Minimus's "Comrade Napoleon" in Chapter 8; Napoleon installed in Snowball's place at the Battle of the Cowshed; the mural placing Napoleon "in the forefront".

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 6–8 had rewritten the principles, Chapters 8–9 install the cult — the replacement anthem "Comrade Napoleon" consuming the founding-revolutionary slot, the Cowshed mural rewriting Napoleon into the bravery the reader watched Snowball perform, and the past now no longer the standard against which the regime could be measured but the regime's own preferred fiction.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys replacement anthem paired with retroactive mural — "Beasts of England" abolished (Chapter 7) and replaced by Minimus's "Comrade Napoleon" (Chapter 8) with its prophetic register "Friend of fatherless! / Fountain of happiness! / Lord of the swill-bucket!", while the historical record is rewritten so that Napoleon, "with the cry of 'Death to Humanity!' had sprung forward and sunk his teeth in Jones's leg" — combining the replacement anthem (the founding revolutionary expression "Beasts of England" erased and replaced by the cult song, the emotional register of the revolution captured by the cult that betrays it) with retroactive mural (the rewriting of the Battle of the Cowshed installing Napoleon into Snowball's documented bravery, the visual record forged to align with the regime's preferred narrative).

• Close analysis: The exclamation marks of "Friend of fatherless! / Fountain of happiness!" register the sycophantic register of the cult — the punctuation enacting the absurd inflation the cataloguing satire is engineered to expose — while the pluperfect "had sprung forward" in the rewritten Cowshed narrative converts what did not happen into what is now to be remembered as having happened, the grammar performing the falsification.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the linguistic capture as complete — the founding anthem replaced and the founding battle rewritten in the same two chapters, the regime now controlling both the emotional vocabulary and the heroic record of the revolution.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the rewriting's terminal achievement — that the regime has captured not only the present and the future but the past, and that the citizens of the regime no longer have a standard against which to measure it because the standard is now the regime's preferred fiction.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the regime's most lethal achievement is the capture of the past — that a regime which has rewritten the standard is a regime that the citizens of the subsequent generation cannot resist because they cannot name it as different from what it replaced, and that the citizens' defence of the original record is therefore the prophylactic against the regime that requires the past to be its own preferred narrative.

• Context (AO3): Stalin's official titles by the late 1930s included "Brilliant Genius of Humanity", "Father of Nations", "Gardener of Human Happiness", and the Short Course (1938) installed Stalin in Lenin's historical place as the founding revolutionary — the replacement anthem and the rewritten Cowshed mural condense those operations into the same two chapters of the novella.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell anatomises the rewriting of history as the regime's structural instrument — Chapter 5 erasing Snowball, Chapters 6–8 rewriting the commandments, Chapters 8–9 installing the cult through replacement anthem and retroactive mural.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 5 installed as the erasure of the visionary, Chapters 6–8 escalated as the rewriting of the standard, Chapters 8–9 consummated as the capture of the heroic past — three operations of the same instrument, the language of accountability captured by the language of the regime.

• Author's purpose: Writing the same year as "Politics and the English Language" (April 1946), Orwell uses the rewriting of history to dramatise, at the level of fictional incident, the linguistic operation the essay would diagnose at the level of analysis — the citizens' defence of clear thought and accurate record as the prophylactic against the regime that requires the past to be its own preferred fiction.

• Ultimate moral: The reader who closes the novella with the rewritten Cowshed mural and the replacement anthem lodged in memory has been trained to demand of every regime the original text against which the regime can be measured — the analytical move that protects every subsequent revolution from the new Manor Farm.

T5 — Fear and Violence

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about fear and violence in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what different characters do

- how Orwell presents ideas about fear and violence.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Fear and violence are the regime's permanent foundation — installed through the secret training of the dogs in Chapter 3, escalated through the public executions in Chapter 7, and consummated through the institutional violence of the closing chapters. The plan tracks three stages — covert preparation, public spectacle, institutional invisibility.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — every stage has a precise NKVD-historical anchor (founded 1934 from OGPU; Great Terror 1936–38; Moscow show trials) AND a universal political archetype (the security apparatus the regime cultivates in private; the pedagogy of public terror; the institutional invisibility of normalised violence). Land in BP1 or the conclusion.

Key technique terms:

• Allegorical mapping — the dogs = the NKVD, the Soviet secret police; the public executions = the Moscow show trials and the Great Terror.

• Auditory escalation — the dogs' "baying" arriving before the bodies; the screams of the executed.

• Ekphrastic violence — Orwell's plain prose registering the throat-tearing without protective metaphor.

• Symbolic preparation — Napoleon takes the puppies "to make himself responsible for their education" (Chapter 3).

• Choric inversion — the sheep's bleating drowning out the dissent the violence is engineered to suppress.

• Spatial public spectacle — the killing knoll, the public execution site of Chapter 7.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Soviet NKVD secret police, founded in 1934 from the predecessor OGPU, conducted the Great Terror of 1936–38 that imprisoned approximately 1.5 million people, executed approximately 700,000 of them, and ran the Moscow show trials at which Zinoviev and Kamenev (1936) and Bukharin (1938) confessed under torture to fabricated charges of treason — the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the dogs' throat-tearing of the confessing animals on the floor of the big barn.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had served in the POUM militia in Spain in 1936–37 and watched Stalinist forces suppress the POUM in Barcelona's May Days of 1937, had direct biographical experience of the operations the novella would subsequently dramatise — the Homage to Catalonia (1938) record of those weeks providing the testimonial spine that Animal Farm converts into beast fable.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 3 covert preparation in which Napoleon takes the puppies and trains them in private, the Chapter 7 public spectacle in which the dogs tear the throats of the confessing animals on the killing knoll, and the Chapters 8–10 institutional invisibility in which the violence becomes ambient — whips carried openly, fear so internalised that coercion is no longer visibly necessary — Orwell presents fear and violence as the regime's permanent foundation, the institutional spine without which every other operation would collapse.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 3: covert preparation (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Napoleon takes the puppies away from their mothers "to make himself responsible for their education".

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs the regime's violence not as the regime's first move but as its precondition — the secret training of the dogs in Chapter 3 occurring while the rest of the farm believes the revolution has already been won, the violence prepared in private long before its public deployment.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys euphemism inside spatial isolation — "Napoleon took them away from their mothers, saying that he would make himself responsible for their education, and put them in a loft so secluded that the rest of the farm soon forgot their existence" (Chapter 3) — combining euphemism (the noun "education" converting militarisation into pedagogy, the regime's preferred linguistic register pre-applied to the operation it conceals) with spatial isolation (the loft "so secluded" removing the violence from public view, the geography itself the precondition of the deployment to come) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The pluperfect "had taken them away" and the closing "soon forgot their existence" register the collective forgetting that the regime's violence requires — the violence prepared in the same chapter as the milk and apples appropriation, the corruption and the violence both seeded in the revolution's first weeks.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the dramatic irony of the scene — the puppies' future use as terror-instruments visible to the reader before the animals can recognise the preparation — generating mounting dread the rest of the novella will subsequently confirm.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural method of totalitarian violence — that the violence is not improvisational but premeditated, the instruments of state terror assembled in secret long before they are deployed.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the totalitarian regime's violence is the regime's foundation rather than its accessory — that no regime sustains itself on rhetoric alone, that the dogs are required from the first chapter onward, and that the citizens of any post-revolutionary regime must demand transparency over the security apparatus the new leadership cultivates.

• Context (AO3): The OGPU, founded in 1923 as the successor to the Cheka and reorganised as the NKVD in 1934, was cultivated under Stalin's personal supervision across the 1920s — the institution Stalin used in 1936–38 for the Great Terror was the institution he had built in private during the years his rivals focused on policy debates.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 7: public spectacle (7 sentences — about 235 words)

Scene focus: the public executions in the big barn; the four young pigs and the hens confess and are torn apart by the dogs.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 3 had installed the violence as covert preparation, Chapter 7 stages it as public spectacle — the executions in the big barn the regime's pedagogy, the killed animals not merely victims but lessons, teaching every survivor that the cost of dissent is destruction.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys ekphrastic plain prose paired with the regime's terrible voice — "When they had finished their confession, the dogs promptly tore their throats out, and in a terrible voice Napoleon demanded whether any other animal had anything to confess" (Chapter 7) — combining ekphrastic plain prose (the verb "tore" registering the violence in lucid Anglo-Saxon vocabulary, the adverb "promptly" converting the killing into procedural efficiency) with the regime's terrible voice (Napoleon's "terrible voice" converting the spectacle into pedagogy, the demand for further confession transforming the survivors into the regime's permanent audience) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The conjunction "and" linking confession to execution registers the impossibility of mercy within the sentence structure itself — the syntax enacting the procedural efficiency it is naming — while the verb "demanded" converts voluntary confession into compelled performance, the regime's pedagogy operating inside the grammar that records it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the executions as the regime's pedagogical climax — the violence not random but designed, every survivor instructed, the killing knoll the geography of the lesson.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the structural function of the spectacle — that the public execution is not merely cruelty but communication, the killed animals the medium through which the regime addresses every survivor across the rest of the chapters.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be demonstrating that the totalitarian regime's violence operates as pedagogy rather than as punishment — that the executions are designed not to remove the executed but to instruct the survivors, and that the survivors' silence across the remaining chapters is therefore the lesson successfully learnt.

• Context (AO3): The Moscow show trials of 1936–38 — Zinoviev and Kamenev (August 1936), Pyatakov and Radek (January 1937), Bukharin and Yagoda (March 1938) — saw the original Bolshevik leadership confess under torture to fabricated charges of treason and be executed by the NKVD; the public spectacle was designed to terrorise the entire population, and Chapter 7's executions condense the operation into a single afternoon on the killing knoll.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 8–10: institutional invisibility (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the whip in Napoleon's trotter (Chapter 10); the dogs gambolling around him; the institutional violence so internalised that coercion is no longer visibly necessary.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 7 had staged the violence as public spectacle, Chapters 8–10 consummate it as institutional invisibility — the violence no longer requiring spectacle because the lesson has been learnt, the whip in Napoleon's trotter and the dogs gambolling beside him the visual condensation of a regime that has internalised its violence into the routine.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys symbolic action inside reversed verb register — "Out came Napoleon himself, majestically upright, casting haughty glances from side to side, and with his dogs gambolling round him. He carried a whip in his trotter" (Chapter 10) — combining symbolic action (the whip in the trotter condensing the entire allegory's argument into a single visual image, the revolutionary now physically the master) with reversed verb register (the verb "gambolling" applied to the dogs that had previously "dashed" and "baying", the apparatus of terror domesticated into pageantry) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The adverb "majestically" and the adjective "haughty" register the transfer of human aristocratic vocabulary onto the boar — the linguistic completion of the visual transformation — while the dogs' "gambolling" registers the institutional violence as the regime's permanent pet, the threat no longer requiring the verbs of attack because the lesson of Chapter 7 has rendered the threat structural.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the violence's institutional consummation — the whip carried openly and the dogs domesticated into courtiers the visual form of a regime whose violence has become so embedded that it no longer requires the spectacles of Chapter 7.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the controlling concept's most lethal implication — that the regime's success is measured by the invisibility of its violence, and that the citizens whose silence the regime has secured are the citizens who can no longer name the violence as violence.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the regime's violence is most lethal when it has become institutional — that the whip in the trotter is the regime's announcement that the spectacle is no longer required, and that the citizens' apparent calm is therefore the structural form of the regime's victory rather than evidence of its absence.

• Context (AO3): By the late 1940s the Soviet Union had embedded the NKVD's successor (the MGB, then the KGB after 1954) into the structure of the state to the point that the spectacular show trials of the 1930s had been replaced by the routine bureaucratic terror Solzhenitsyn would document in The Gulag Archipelago (1973) — the institutional invisibility the closing chapters of Animal Farm anticipate.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell stages fear and violence as the regime's permanent foundation — Chapter 3 covert preparation, Chapter 7 public spectacle, Chapters 8–10 institutional invisibility.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 3 installed as the puppies' secret education, Chapter 7 escalated as the throat-tearing on the killing knoll, Chapters 8–10 consummated as the whip in the trotter — three stages of the same operation, the violence the institutional spine without which the rewriting and the appropriation would collapse.

• Author's purpose: Writing from his own Spanish experience of watching Stalinist forces suppress the POUM in Barcelona in 1937, Orwell uses the violence arc to demonstrate that the totalitarian regime's foundation is the security apparatus the leadership cultivates in private, and that the citizens of any post-revolutionary regime must demand transparency over that apparatus before it acquires the institutional invisibility that renders it unaccountable.

• Ultimate moral: The whip in the trotter is the regime's announcement that the lesson has been learnt — and the reader who recognises the visual condensation has been trained to demand of every leadership the transparency over the security apparatus that the citizens of Animal Farm did not.

T6 — Animalism and The Failure of Ideals

Question

How does Orwell present the corruption of Animalism's ideals in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what Animalism originally promises

- how Orwell uses the corruption of Animalism's ideals to convey his ideas about the revolution.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Animalism is the revolutionary vision Old Major articulates in Chapter 1, codified into the Seven Commandments in Chapter 2, and progressively betrayed by the pigs from the milk-and-apples appropriation onward — the gap between the original ideals and the lived actuality the novella's central political argument. The plan tracks three stages — Animalism articulated, Animalism codified, Animalism betrayed.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The double allegorical engine — Animalism condenses Marx's Communist Manifesto (1848) and Lenin's State and Revolution (1917) into the Chapter 1 address, with Stalin's 1936 Constitution as the historical analogue of the rewritten commandments. Land in BP3 or the conclusion.

Key technique terms:

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses ("Man is the only…"); the rhetorical engine of the original diagnosis.

• Codification — the conversion of oral vision into public text; the Seven Commandments painted on the barn wall.

• Hamartia — error in action (Aristotle, Poetics); Old Major's failure to specify accountability mechanisms is the structural error the rest of the novella enacts.

• Cyclical structure — Manor Farm renamed Animal Farm renamed Manor Farm; the form is the failure.

• Anthem replacement — "Beasts of England" (Chapter 1) abolished and replaced by "Comrade Napoleon" (Chapter 8).

• Final oxymoron — "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others" (Chapter 10) — the founding principle and its inversion in the same sentence.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Karl Marx published The Communist Manifesto in February 1848 with the rallying cry "Workers of the world, unite! You have nothing to lose but your chains" — the diagnostic case for the dismantling of capitalist exploitation that, by Vladimir Lenin's return to Russia in April 1917, had become the operational programme of the Bolsheviks who took power in the October Revolution of the same year.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who described himself as a "democratic socialist" in "Why I Write" (1946), was therefore not writing a critique of the revolutionary principle but a warning that the principle would be progressively betrayed by any leadership that did not specify, in advance, the accountability mechanisms by which the new regime would be checked — the gap between Marx's diagnosis and Stalin's regime the historical record the novella condenses into fable.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter 1 articulation of Animalism in Old Major's diagnostic speech, the Chapter 2 codification of the Seven Commandments in "great white letters" on the barn wall, and the Chapters 6–10 betrayal of every principle through the rewritten commandments, the closing dinner, and the renamed Manor Farm, Orwell presents the failure of ideals as the structural consequence of unaccountable revolution — the original vision honourable, the codification institutional, the betrayal historical and inevitable in the absence of the accountability mechanism Old Major's vision did not specify.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapter 1: Animalism articulated (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Old Major addresses the assembled animals in the big barn; the diagnosis of human exploitation; the teaching of "Beasts of England".

• Topic sentence: Orwell installs Animalism's ideals as the moral baseline of the novella — Old Major's Chapter 1 address articulating the case for revolution with the lucid force of authentic political analysis, the diagnosis honourable and the case for revolution therefore not refutable by the betrayal that follows.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys anaphora inside catalogued accusation — Old Major's "Man is the only creature that consumes without producing. He does not give milk, he does not lay eggs, he is too weak to pull the plough, he cannot run fast enough to catch rabbits. Yet he is lord of all the animals" (Chapter 1) — combining anaphora (the repeated "He does not… he does not… he is too weak… he cannot" sequence cataloguing human incapacity through grammatical accumulation) with antithesis (the catalogue of incapacity set against the closing "Yet he is lord of all the animals", the contradiction supplying the revolutionary case in a single rhetorical step) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "he" repeated through the catalogue refuses Man the dignity of personhood, returning the linguistic violence Man has performed on the animals back through the syntax — while the conjunction "Yet" names the structural injustice in a single monosyllabic word, the smallness of the conjunction matched only by the size of the case it carries.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the case for revolution articulated with the lucid force of authentic political analysis — the speech persuasive in itself, the persuasiveness essential to the controlling concept Orwell is defending.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers Orwell's most precise structural choice — the ideals are honourable, and the betrayal that follows is therefore not the refutation of the original vision but the warning that the vision must be defended against the leadership it produces.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be insisting through Major's address that anti-Stalinism and anti-socialism are not the same position — that the diagnosis Marx published in 1848 retains its force regardless of the betrayal Stalin enacted, and that the reader who confuses the two has misread both the speech and the novella.

• Context (AO3): Karl Marx's The Communist Manifesto (February 1848) opens with "a spectre is haunting Europe" and proceeds through a structural analysis of capitalist exploitation — the address Old Major delivers in Chapter 1 condenses the Manifesto's diagnostic move into a single page, and Orwell's self-description as "democratic socialist" ("Why I Write", 1946) confirms the diagnosis is not what the novella is satirising.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapter 2: Animalism codified (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the Seven Commandments are painted on the barn wall in "great white letters" immediately after the revolution.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 1 had articulated the ideals, Chapter 2 codifies them — the conversion of Old Major's oral vision into public, durable text the procedural form of accountability the ideals require, the codification the institutional move the revolution requires to outlive the visionary who articulated it.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys the codification of the principles paired with the visual permanence of the public text — "They had been painted on the tarred wall in great white letters that could be read thirty yards away" and "there, in unalterable form, were the rules by which the animals of Animal Farm must live for ever after" (Chapter 2) — combining the codification of the principles (the conversion of the oral vision into public text, the institutional move the revolution requires) with the visual permanence of the public text (the "great white letters" and the spatial measure "thirty yards away" registering the principles as visible to every animal at every moment, the public visibility the procedural form of accountability the principles require) inside the same passage.

• Close analysis: The phrase "in unalterable form" registers the principles' precondition — the assumption that the form is unalterable — while the temporal "for ever after" exposes, retrospectively, the gap the regime will exploit: the assumption of permanence is precisely what permits the rewriting that follows, the form unalterable only as long as no one is permitted to alter it.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the institutional achievement of the codification — the ideals installed in public text, visible to every animal, the procedural form of accountability that the gap Old Major's vision left required.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader registers the structural fragility — the ideals installed at the level of text but not at the level of literacy, the public text only as durable as the animals' capacity to read it, and the literacy gap therefore the gap through which the rewriting will subsequently operate.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the institutional codification of the ideals is the necessary but insufficient condition of accountability — that the public text requires not only its installation but the cognitive equipment by which the masses can defend it, and that any revolution that codifies the principles without distributing the literacy is the revolution whose ideals can be rewritten without the masses noticing.

• Context (AO3): The 1918 Constitution of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic codified universal suffrage, the abolition of class distinctions, and the principle of "All Power to the Soviets" — yet by the early 1920s the soviets had been effectively replaced by Communist Party committees, the gap between the codified ideals and the institutional reality the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the barn wall.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 6–10: Animalism betrayed (7 sentences — about 240 words)

Scene focus: the rewritten commandments across Chapters 6–8; the closing dinner; the renamed Manor Farm; the final oxymoron "All animals are equal but some animals are more equal than others".

• Topic sentence: Where Chapter 2 had codified the ideals, Chapters 6–10 stage their betrayal — the commandments rewritten one by one to license every transgression, the closing dinner allying the pigs with the very humans the revolution was launched to overthrow, and the final oxymoron consummating the gap between the original ideals and the lived actuality the novella has been measuring for ten chapters.

• Technique + evidence: Orwell deploys cumulative rewriting paired with final oxymoron — "There was nothing there now except a single Commandment. It ran: ALL ANIMALS ARE EQUAL BUT SOME ANIMALS ARE MORE EQUAL THAN OTHERS" (Chapter 10) — combining cumulative rewriting (the seven commandments collapsed into a single line, the conclusion of the operation that began with the rewriting of "No animal shall sleep in a bed" into "No animal shall sleep in a bed with sheets" in Chapter 6) with oxymoron (the contradictory yoking of "equal" and "more equal", the comparative rendering the absolute meaningless within the same sentence) inside the wall's final inscription.

• Close analysis: The comparative "more equal" exposes the regime's most audacious move precisely because the comparative cannot grammatically modify an absolute — "equal" admits no degrees — and the impossibility of the formulation registers, at the level of grammar, the impossibility of the regime the formulation licenses, the capitalised typography enacting the regime's appropriation of the public space where the original ideals were once installed.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the collapse of the codification as the structural form of the controlling concept's claim — the ideals consumed by the regime that was installed to defend them, the wall now the regime's billboard rather than the revolution's constitution.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the betrayal as the inevitable consequence of the hamartia — that Old Major's vision did not specify the mechanism by which the leadership it produced would be held accountable, and that the failure of ideals is therefore not personal vice but structural inevitability.

• Author's purpose: Orwell may be arguing that the failure of ideals is not the refutation of the ideals but the warning that ideals require the institutional design to outlive the visionary — that the diagnosis Marx published is honourable, the codification the soviets installed was institutional, and the betrayal Stalin enacted was the structural consequence of the accountability mechanism the original visionaries did not specify.

• Context (AO3): Stalin's 1936 Constitution preserved the rhetoric of Soviet democracy — universal suffrage, civil liberties, freedom of speech — while installing the centralised personal authority of the General Secretary; the gap between the codified Constitution and the Great Terror operating beneath it is the historical pattern Orwell condenses into the single line on the wall in Chapter 10.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Orwell anatomises the failure of Animalism's ideals as the structural consequence of unaccountable revolution — Chapter 1 articulating the vision, Chapter 2 codifying the principles, Chapters 6–10 betraying them through rewriting and renaming.

• Controlling concept: What Chapter 1 installed as Old Major's honourable diagnosis, Chapter 2 codified as the public Seven Commandments, Chapters 6–10 betrayed as the rewritten commandments and the renamed Manor Farm — three stages of the same vision, the consistency confirming Orwell's claim that the original ideals are not what the novella satirises but what it defends.

• Author's purpose: Writing as a self-described democratic socialist ("Why I Write", 1946) for a 1945 readership about to elect Attlee's Labour government on a platform of welfare and nationalisation, Orwell uses the failure of ideals to argue that the principle of equality is honourable and that the institutional design must be specific enough to defend the principle against the leadership the principle produces.

• Ultimate moral: The reader who closes the novella with the final oxymoron lodged in memory has been trained to demand of every revolution the accountability mechanism Old Major's vision did not specify — the analytical move that protects every subsequent set of ideals from the new Manor Farm.

T7 — Loyalty and Betrayal

Question

How does Orwell explore ideas about loyalty and betrayal in Animal Farm?

Write about:

- what different characters do

- how Orwell presents ideas about loyalty and betrayal.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Loyalty and betrayal are the novella's central moral and political pairing — Boxer's two maxims register the working class's loyalty, Snowball's expulsion registers the visionary intelligentsia's betrayal, and the closing dinner registers the betrayal consummated as alliance with the very enemies the original loyalty was pledged against. The plan tracks three stages — loyalty installed (Chapter 3), loyalty extracted (Chapters 5–8), loyalty betrayed (Chapters 9–10).

Pedagogical depth to thread. The universal political archetype — the regime requires loyalty as the resource extraction it cannot survive without; betrayal is therefore not the regime's failure but its structural fulfilment. Land in BP3 or the conclusion.

Key technique terms:

• Maxim — short formulaic statement; Boxer's "I will work harder" (Chapter 3) and "Napoleon is always right" (Chapter 5).

• Foil pairing — Boxer's loyalty against Mollie's defection; Snowball's principled exile against Squealer's purchased fidelity.

• Symbolic betrayal — the closing dinner with Pilkington; the alliance with the human enemy.

• Dramatic irony — the reader recognises the betrayal seconds before the loyal animals do.

• Bathetic specificity — the side of the knacker's van condensing the regime's transactional betrayal into a proper noun.

• Cyclical return — the renamed Manor Farm registering the betrayal as completed cycle.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 120 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Molotov-Ribbentrop Pact of August 1939 saw Stalin sign a non-aggression treaty with Adolf Hitler's Nazi Germany after twenty years of Soviet propaganda that had identified fascism as the global working class's existential enemy — the betrayal so audacious that British Communist Party membership halved within weeks, and the historical pattern Orwell would condense into Napoleon's closing dinner with Pilkington and Frederick.

• Building sentence (historical context): Orwell, who had served in the POUM militia in Spain in 1936–37 and watched Stalinist forces suppress his own faction in Barcelona's May Days, was therefore writing from direct biographical experience of the regime's capacity to betray the loyalty it had explicitly extracted — and the working-class loyalty Boxer registers across the novella is the loyalty Orwell had watched the Spanish Civil War consume in the same operations.
